proposals.title: Synthetic Neuroevolution of Predictive Control: A Causal Test of the Elaboration Hypothesis Using Engineered Reflexive Archetypes
proposals.motivation: A central tenet of active inference and predictive coding is that sophisticated, predictive brains evolved from simpler, reflexive control systems. However, in biological systems, this 'elaboration hypothesis' is entangled with evolutionary history, making causal validation impossible. A synthetic testbed is needed to isolate and test the principles governing how predictive control can be built upon non-predictive substrates, directly informing models of brain regional specialization and hierarchical systems in health and disease.
proposals.hypothesis: When a mutable predictive circuit is introduced into a robust, evolved reflexive control system, it will parasitize and functionally re-purpose the existing reflexive architecture to minimize free energy, rather than creating a duplicate or independent control pathway.
proposals.method: We will construct a simulated agent-environment testbed using Markov Decision Processes. First, neuroevolution (e.g., NEAT or policy gradient methods) will evolve agents with fixed, hardwired sensorimotor reflexes to solve reactive survival tasks. Second, we will introduce a plastic, generative-model-based circuit (a simple recurrent neural network or Dynamic Causal Model-like module) with parameters that can be tuned via variational free energy minimization. Crucially, the environment will then be altered to introduce latent temporal structure, rewarding anticipation. We will track the causal interactions and functional connectivity between the reflexive and predictive subsystems throughout learning.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first causal, empirical evidence for a functional pathway from reflexive to predictive control, a foundational idea in theoretical neuroscience. It will generate formal, testable predictions about neural circuit changes during the progression of diseases like Alzheimer's, where predictive processing is degraded. The resulting synthetic models will serve as 'digital twins' for testing hypotheses about brain systems and control.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its adversarial, two-stage evolutionary design, which first engineers successful non-predictive archetypes to serve as a fixed substrate, then explicitly tests how predictive circuits colonize this substrate—a clean inversion of typical AI approaches that start with predictive models.

proposals.title: Statistical Breakdown of the Reflex-to-Prediction Transition: A Free Energy Principle Perspective on Small-N Ensemble Failure
proposals.motivation: The free energy principle posits that adaptive agents minimize variational free energy, transitioning from reflexive to predictive control as a normative imperative. However, this assumes aggregated reflexive dynamics yield a stable statistical manifold for prediction. In neurological disease and across brain regions, small, heterogeneous populations of hardwired circuits may violate the Central Limit Theorem, creating a fundamental obstacle to this normative transition that remains uncharacterized.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the failure of small-N reflexive ensembles to converge to a stable 'power average' defines a precise statistical boundary condition where predictive models cannot reliably emerge, necessitating alternative architectures like meta-control or model selection.
proposals.method: We will construct generative models of small-N heterogeneous reflexive circuits using stochastic differential equations. Using Dynamic Causal Modelling (DCM) and Bayesian model reduction, we will simulate their aggregated output and formally test for convergence failures (e.g., via analysis of Lévy stable distributions). We will then fit these models to empirical intracranial EEG and MEG data from Alzheimer's disease cohorts, mapping regions where spectral properties correlate with predicted statistical breakdowns.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal, statistical foundation for limits of predictive processing, directly informing theories of cognitive architecture. It will generate testable biomarkers for neurological conditions where predictive integration fails, linking microcircuit pathology to macroscale dysregulation.
proposals.novelty: It applies rigorous limit theorems of statistics to a core tenet of the free energy principle, moving from a normative 'should' to a mechanistic 'when,' which is a novel, falsifiable constraint on predictive brain theories.

proposals.title: Cortically-Constrained Active Inference: Validating Duplicated Sensorimotor Loops with Human Source-Localised Oscillatory Dynamics
proposals.motivation: Current computational models of sensorimotor control, including those based on active inference, often lack a principled link to the spectro-spatial dynamics of the human brain. This makes it difficult to assess their neurobiological plausibility and limits their utility for understanding the pathophysiology of movement disorders like Parkinson's disease, where specific oscillatory signatures (e.g., beta-band aberrations) are central. A rigorous, model-based bridge between behaviour and source-localised neurophysiology is therefore critical for global brain health exposomics.
proposals.hypothesis: An active inference agent, whose hierarchical generative model architecture and message-passing dynamics are explicitly constrained by the anatomical and oscillatory features of the human sensorimotor hierarchy, will not only replicate goal-directed behaviour but will also necessarily reproduce the specific, source-localised spectral features (e.g., beta-band power modulations) observed in human MEG/EEG during equivalent tasks.
proposals.method: We will construct a hierarchical active inference agent using the SPM software suite, implementing duplicated (e.g., fast/slow) predictive loops for sensorimotor control. Its network architecture (node connectivity and time constants) will be designed to map onto the human sensorimotor cortices as defined by source-localised MEG/EEG parcellations. Using the Parametric Empirical Bayes framework and Dynamic Causal Modelling (DCM), we will fit the agent's internal dynamics to spectro-spatial features (power, cross-spectral density) extracted from human MEG data during a precision-grip task. The agent's behavioural repertoire will then be expanded, and its emergent oscillatory dynamics will be validated against held-out human data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, falsifiable methodology for grounding computational psychiatry and neurology models in human neurophysiology. It will yield a validated, neuro-mechanistic model of sensorimotor loops that can be perturbed to simulate disease states, offering a direct link between algorithmic descriptions of behaviour and their implementation in cortical circuits. This advances the field from correlation to mechanistic, model-based explanation.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies not in building an active inference agent, but in using source-localised human oscillatory dynamics as a simultaneous architectural constraint and validation target, creating a closed loop where behavioural model plausibility is contingent on reproducing specific, non-trivial spectro-spatial features of whole-brain activity.

proposals.title: A Phylogenetic Free-Energy Framework: Reconstructing the Evolution of Predictive Coding Dynamics Across Primates
proposals.motivation: Understanding the evolution of neural computations like predictive coding is a central challenge in neuroscience. While comparative neuroanatomy and phylogenetic methods exist, they lack a formal link to the trial-by-trial dynamics described by active inference and state-space models. Bridging this gap is essential to move from correlating brain regions to explaining the evolutionary trajectory of core computational principles underlying cognition and its disorders.
proposals.hypothesis: That the latent states governing trial-wise neural dynamics (e.g., prediction error precision) evolve under phylogenetic constraints, and their ancestral trajectories can be statistically inferred, revealing a conserved evolutionary sequence in the implementation of hierarchical predictive processing.
proposals.method: We will develop a hierarchical Bayesian model integrating a phylogenetic generalized linear mixed model (PGLMM) with a Kalman filter or variational filter state-space estimator. Trial-level magnetoencephalography (MEG) or intracranial EEG spectral data from multiple primate species during a mismatch negativity paradigm will serve as observations. The latent states (e.g., prediction error magnitude, precision) will be modelled as evolving under a Brownian motion or Ornstein-Uhlenbeck process along the known species phylogeny, using the PGLMM to handle phylogenetic non-independence and the state-space model to de-noise trial-level dynamics.
proposals.contribution: This will provide the first formal, quantitative method for reconstructing ancestral neurocomputational states, moving evolutionary neuroscience beyond static anatomy. It will generate testable predictions about the evolutionary ordering of changes in predictive coding parameters, offering a new lens through which to interpret interspecies differences in cognitive architectures and their potential vulnerability to disease.
proposals.novelty: The fusion of state-space models for trial-level neural dynamics with phylogenetic comparative methods is novel, creating a generative model that can statistically reconstruct the evolution of real-time computational processes, not just static traits.

proposals.title: Stochastic Optimal Control for Precision Mode-Matching: A Formal Synthesis of Active Inference and Control Theory
proposals.motivation: Active inference provides a unified Bayesian mechanics for perception and action, but its implementation often relies on heuristic gradient descents on variational free energy. This lacks the formal guarantees and efficiency of established stochastic optimal control (SOC) theory. Bridging this gap is critical for developing robust, scalable models of brain function and for engineering advanced, brain-inspired autonomous systems that can operate reliably in uncertain environments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the mode-matching and active inference problems can be formally cast as stochastic optimal control problems, where the expected free energy functional serves as a cost-to-go, and that solving them via SOC methods (e.g., path integral control) will yield more efficient and neurally plausible solutions than generic variational inference schemes.
proposals.method: We will formulate the active inference state-space model within a stochastic differential equation framework. The expected free energy will be recast as a cost function for a finite-horizon SOC problem. We will employ the path integral control formalism to derive an optimal control law, leveraging the Feynman-Kac theorem to transform the Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman equation into a sampling problem. This will be implemented numerically using iterative importance sampling, comparing its performance and neural interpretability to standard variational message passing in generative models of sensorimotor loops and whole-brain neurophysiological data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous control-theoretic foundation for active inference, offering new formal insights into optimality and efficiency in brain-like computation. It will deliver a novel class of scalable algorithms for simulating and inverting large-scale hierarchical models, directly applicable to understanding regional brain dynamics in health and disease, such as the loss of precision control in Alzheimer's disease.
proposals.novelty: While control theory and active inference are often discussed in parallel, a direct formal synthesis casting expected free energy minimization as a stochastic optimal control problem and solving it with path integral methods is a novel, non-obvious integration that moves beyond conceptual analogies to a unified computational theory.

proposals.title: A Reservoir Computing Model of Hierarchical Belief Dynamics: Linking Regional Neurotransmitter Gradients to Pathological Inference in Alzheimer's Disease
proposals.motivation: The Free Energy Principle posits that the brain performs hierarchical Bayesian inference, with belief dynamics emerging from recurrent neural interactions. However, the mechanistic link between regional neurophysiological heterogeneity—evident in neurotransmitter receptor gradients—and the breakdown of these dynamics in diseases like Alzheimer's remains unclear. A computational framework is needed to model how such emergent belief updating fails, moving beyond static network descriptions.
proposals.hypothesis: The dynamics of hierarchical belief updating can be modelled as a reservoir computing system, where the fixed, heterogeneous recurrent connectivity of the reservoir—informed by empirical neurotransmitter receptor topographies—naturally implements approximate Bayesian inference, and that the progression of Alzheimer's disease can be simulated as a targeted degradation of this reservoir's dynamics.
proposals.method: We will construct a Echo State Network (ESN) as the core reservoir, where the fixed internal weight matrix will be structured and scaled using pre-2026 human neurotransmitter receptor density maps (e.g., for GABA, glutamate) to impose biologically plausible regional computational constraints. Inputs will be synthetic sensory sequences with hidden statistical regularities. The readout layer, trained via ridge regression, will map the reservoir's transient states to explicit belief reports (e.g., posterior expectations). Disease progression will be modelled by progressively lesioning reservoir nodes or connections in a topography-informed manner, mirroring neuropathological spread.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, mechanistic model of belief dynamics as an emergent property of a biologically constrained recurrent system, directly linking molecular-scale heterogeneity to systems-level inference. It will offer a new computational tool—a 'pathological inference engine'—to generate testable hypotheses for the neurophysiological progression observed in Alzheimer's disease via techniques like Dynamic Causal Modelling.
proposals.novelty: While reservoir computing has been used for time-series prediction, its application to explicitly model hierarchical Bayesian belief updating under biologically derived architectural constraints is novel. This bridges the gap between the abstract mathematics of active inference and the dynamical systems perspective of computational neuroscience.

proposals.title: A Stochastic Calculus of Sentience: Continuous-Time Active Inference for Whole-Body Physiology and Brain Dynamics
proposals.motivation: Active inference, a unifying theory of brain function, has been predominantly formulated for discrete states and time steps. This limits its application to real-world biological processes, such as whole-body physiological regulation and high-frequency neural dynamics, which are inherently continuous in nature. A continuous-time formulation is essential for integrating multi-scale exposome data and modelling the neurophysiological progression of diseases like Alzheimer's, where pathological changes unfold over continuous time.
proposals.hypothesis: That the principles of variational free energy minimization can be generalized to continuous state spaces and continuous-time processes using stochastic calculus, providing a more natural and mathematically rigorous description of embodied perception and action.
proposals.method: We will develop the formulation using stochastic differential equations (SDEs) to describe the dynamics of hidden states and beliefs. The free energy functional will be defined as an integral over time, leading to a continuous-time update rule for recognition dynamics (a generalized filtering equation). We will implement this using a hierarchical generative model based on generalized coordinates of motion, with actions derived from the gradient of expected free energy expressed as a cost-to-go function. Numerical solutions will employ stochastic integration schemes (e.g., Euler-Maruyama) and be tested on simulated data of coupled neuro-vascular oscillations.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a foundational mathematical extension of active inference, enabling direct coupling with continuous physiological models and real-time neuroimaging data (e.g., M/EEG). It will offer new tools for dynamic causal modelling of disease trajectories and for simulating agent-environment interactions in real time, bridging a critical gap between theoretical neuroscience and systems biology.
proposals.novelty: While continuous-time formulations exist for predictive coding, a full active inference scheme—integrating perception, planning, and action in continuous time and state spaces—has not been rigorously derived. This moves beyond discretized approximations to a native treatment of temporal continuity.

proposals.title: Doubt and Discovery: A Generative Model of Meta-Learning and Structural Uncertainty in Active Inference
proposals.motivation: Active inference under the free energy principle typically assumes a fixed, veridical generative model structure, an assumption at odds with biological learning and neuropsychiatric conditions like schizophrenia, where model structure is doubted. This 'perfect model alignment' assumption limits the theory's ability to explain how agents discover causal structure in an uncertain world or how such processes fail in disease.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an active inference agent equipped with a hierarchical model over its own model structures will exhibit meta-learning dynamics, where 'doubt' (structural uncertainty) drives exploration and epistemic foraging, and that pathological parameterizations of this meta-model will recapitulate key features of psychotic thought disorder.
proposals.method: We will develop a hierarchical active inference scheme where a 'hypermodel' (a Dirichlet process or Bayesian model average) governs transitions between alternative lower-level generative model architectures. The agent's expected free energy will be augmented with a structural complexity term, and belief updating will operate on both model parameters and model identity. This will be implemented using established variational message passing and dynamic causal modelling (DCM) frameworks, applied to simulated agents and fitted to M/EEG data from healthy controls and early psychosis patients.
proposals.contribution: This work will formally integrate structure learning into active inference, providing a unified account of exploration, curiosity, and model discovery. It will yield a novel computational phenotype for psychosis as a failure to resolve structural uncertainty, linking neurophysiological (DCM) and cognitive (belief updating) levels of description in brain disease.
proposals.novelty: While active inference agents can learn parameters, learning or doubting the graph structure of their generative model is a non-trivial extension. This moves beyond perfect model alignment to formalize 'doubt' as a quantifiable driver of behavior and a potential locus of pathology.

proposals.title: Learning the Generative Likelihood: A Hierarchical Bayesian and Active Inference Account of Sensory Mapping in Health and Disease
proposals.motivation: In active inference and related Bayesian brain theories, the generative model's observation likelihood (linking hidden states to sensory data) is typically assumed fixed and known. This is neurobiologically implausible, as sensory mappings—from receptor densities to cortical hierarchies—are shaped by development, experience, and disease. Pre-2026 work on whole-body-exposome models and receptor topography highlights that ignoring this learnable mapping limits models of neurophysiological progression in conditions like Alzheimer's, where sensory processing deficits are early markers.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an agent's generative model must hierarchically learn its observation likelihood parameters (e.g., precision, gain, and nonlinear mappings) through experience, and that this learning process is governed by the same free energy principles that govern state estimation and policy selection.
proposals.method: We will extend the active inference framework by treating observation model parameters as hidden states at a higher temporal scale, inferred via Bayesian updating or variational message passing. This will be implemented within the SPM/DEM toolbox, using dynamic causal modelling (DCM) for empirical validation. We will apply this to simulated agents and to pre-2026 M/EEG datasets from Alzheimer's disease cohorts, testing if learned likelihood parameters track regional disease progression and explain spectral abnormalities better than fixed-likelihood models.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal computational account of how brains learn 'what to expect' from their sensors, unifying perceptual learning and inference. It will offer a new modelling tool for psychiatry and neurology, where altered sensory likelihoods may underpin symptoms. The resulting framework will bridge cognitive-level theories with systems-level neurophysiological evidence.
proposals.novelty: While prior work optimizes model parameters *for* an experimenter, we formalize the *in-agent* learning of the sensory mapping as an integral part of perception and action. This moves beyond treating the likelihood as a fixed design matrix in DCM, making it a dynamic, context-sensitive part of the generative model.

proposals.title: A Hierarchical Active Inference Framework for Complex Cognitive Control: Modelling Task-Switching and Working Memory Updating
proposals.motivation: Current active inference tutorials and models focus on simple perceptual decision-making, limiting their applicability to real-world cognitive health and disease. To understand the neural mechanisms of higher-order dysfunction in conditions like Alzheimer's disease, we need generative models that can simulate the dynamic, hierarchical control required for complex tasks. Extending the framework to paradigms like task-switching and n-back tasks bridges a critical gap between theoretical neurobiology and clinical cognitive neuroscience.
proposals.hypothesis: The hierarchical organisation of precision-weighted prediction errors, formalised within active inference, provides a sufficient mechanism to explain behavioural and neurophysiological signatures of cognitive control. Specifically, we hypothesise that deficits in modulating prior precision at higher hierarchical levels will reproduce the performance degradation and altered spectral profiles observed in prodromal disease states.
proposals.method: We will extend the standard active inference scheme for perceptual categorization to a hierarchical state-space model with multiple, competing task-sets (e.g., colour vs. shape rules). The update equations for hidden states and precision will be derived using variational Laplace, implemented in SPM12. We will fit the model to both synthetic data and pre-2026 magnetoencephalography (MEG) datasets from healthy controls during a cued task-switching paradigm, using Dynamic Causal Modelling (DCM) and Bayesian model reduction to compare architectures.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a publicly available, tutorial-level active inference model for complex cognition, moving beyond perception to action selection under volatile contexts. It will offer a formal link between computational constructs (e.g., precision) and regional neurophysiological measures (e.g., frontal theta power), generating testable predictions for how control hierarchies break down in early Alzheimer's progression.
proposals.novelty: While active inference has been applied to motor control and perception, its detailed application to canonical cognitive control tasks with a direct link to human neurophysiology is novel. The integration of a multi-level precision hierarchy with established cognitive paradigms to explain disease-relevant spectral perturbations represents a significant advance.

proposals.title: A Hierarchical Gaussian Filter for Whole-Brain Dynamics: Linking Neural Process Theory to Multi-Modal Neuroimaging and Behaviour
proposals.motivation: Current models of brain disease often treat neural dynamics, neurochemistry, and behaviour as separate domains, limiting a mechanistic understanding of how regional pathophysiology unfolds. Integrating Karl Friston's neural process theory—a formal account of neuronal message passing under predictive coding—with empirical multi-modal data (e.g., fMRI, EEG, receptor maps) offers a path to a unified model of brain function and its disintegration. This is critical for moving beyond correlational biomarkers to models that can explain disease progression and individual differences in conditions like Alzheimer's.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the hierarchical Gaussian filter, as an instantiation of neural process theory, can generate synthetic fMRI and EEG spectra that quantitatively match empirical data when its parameters are constrained by regional neurotransmitter receptor densities and its predictions are coupled to a generative model of behavioural task performance.
proposals.method: We will implement a whole-brain hierarchical Gaussian filter, where each node's dynamics are governed by precision-weighted prediction errors. The model's intrinsic (somatic) and extrinsic (synaptic) time constants will be informed by regional receptor density maps (e.g., from PET). We will fit the model to simultaneously acquired resting-state fMRI (capturing slow dynamics) and M/EEG (capturing fast dynamics) data using dynamic causal modelling (DCM) and Bayesian model reduction. Model evidence will be compared against standard DCMs without neural process priors.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a first-principles, biologically constrained model that formally links neurotransmission, large-scale neural dynamics, and spectral phenotypes. It will deliver a novel tool for in-silico experiments, predicting the neurophysiological effects of targeted pharmacological or pathological receptor changes. This advances the goal of computational assays for precision brain health.
proposals.novelty: While neural mass models are common, directly embedding the mathematical constructs of neural process theory (e.g., precision matrices as function of receptor densities) into a whole-brain DCM is novel. This moves from describing neural activity to explaining it in terms of biologically grounded inference, creating a direct bridge between the Free Energy Principle and empirical multi-modal neuroimaging.

proposals.title: A Biologically Plausible, Spiking Neural Network Implementation of Active Inference for Circuit-Level Neuromodulation
proposals.motivation: Active inference, framed as a partially observable Markov decision process (POMDP), provides a unifying theory of brain function, perception, and action. However, its algorithmic implementations remain largely abstract, limiting direct translation to testable neurobiological mechanisms and circuit-level interventions for neurological and psychiatric disease. Bridging this gap is essential for grounding computational psychiatry in the biophysics of neural systems.
proposals.hypothesis: The core POMDP operations of active inference—state estimation, precision weighting, and expected free energy minimization—can be mapped onto canonical microcircuit motifs with distinct cell types and neuromodulatory receptors, creating a functional link between computational theory and regional neurophysiology.
proposals.method: We will construct a spiking neural network using the leaky integrate-and-fire (LIF) formalism, structured according to the predictive coding hierarchy of canonical microcircuits (superficial/deep pyramidal cells and interneurons). Precision (inverse variance) will be encoded by gain modulation via simulated dopaminergic (D1) and cholinergic (M1) receptor dynamics, informed by pre-2026 neurotransmitter receptor atlases. The network will be trained and tested on a classic sensory discrimination task using event-based, biologically constrained learning rules (e.g., spike-timing-dependent plasticity with homeostatic control) to approximate variational message passing.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first biologically detailed, spiking neural network model of a full active inference POMDP, directly linking theoretical constructs like expected free energy to measurable circuit dynamics. It will generate testable predictions for how regional variations in receptor density (e.g., in Alzheimer's disease) alter belief updating and behaviour, providing a new model-based lens for interpreting intracranial EEG and MEG spectra.
proposals.novelty: While abstract neuronal implementations of predictive coding exist, a spiking network that performs active inference—integrating action selection and precision-weighted perception through neuromodulatory control—remains an unsolved challenge. This moves beyond functional analogy to a mechanistic, circuit-level implementation.

proposals.title: Validating Metastable Attractor Dynamics in Human Cortical Hierarchies Using Intracranial EEG and Dynamic Causal Modelling
proposals.motivation: The free energy principle posits that the brain operates as a hierarchical generative model, with metastable attractor dynamics underpinning perception and action. Pre-2026 work using M/EEG has inferred these dynamics, but direct validation of predicted metastable modes at the cortical source level is lacking. Invasive intracranial EEG (iEEG) provides the necessary spatiotemporal resolution to test these fundamental predictions of active inference in the human brain.
proposals.hypothesis: That the spatiotemporal patterns of iEEG spectral activity, particularly in high-gamma and beta bands, will conform to metastable attractor states predicted by whole-brain dynamic causal models (DCMs) of hierarchical message passing, and that these attractor landscapes will be pathologically altered in focal epilepsy.
proposals.method: We will apply spectral DCM to source-localized iEEG data from patients undergoing pre-surgical monitoring for focal epilepsy. Using a canonical microcircuit model, we will estimate subject-specific effective connectivity and ensuing metastable dynamics (via eigenmode decomposition). Predictions for state transitions and spectral fingerprints of these attractors will be tested against empirical iEEG using parametric empirical Bayes and Bayesian model reduction. Crucially, we will compare attractor landscapes in epileptogenic zones versus homologous contralateral regions.
proposals.contribution: This will provide the first direct, within-subject validation of metastable attractor dynamics predicted by active inference at the cortical source level. It will establish a formal link between computational theory and invasive human neurophysiology, and offer a novel model-based biomarker for the altered dynamics of epileptic networks.
proposals.novelty: While DCM has been applied to iEEG, no study has explicitly used it to test the free-energy-derived predictions of metastable attractor states. The novelty lies in the targeted validation of a core theoretical construct using the unique resolution of invasive recordings, moving beyond correlation to model-based prediction.

proposals.title: A Free Energy Principle Account of Cortical Column Dynamics: Linking Microcircuit Pathophysiology to Macroscopic Disease Signatures
proposals.motivation: Current whole-brain models in computational psychiatry, including those using Dynamic Causal Modelling (DCM), often treat brain regions as homogeneous units. This obscures how disease processes like Alzheimer's originate and propagate through specific microcircuit motifs (e.g., canonical laminar circuits). A principled, scale-bridging theory is needed to explain how local synaptic and receptor pathologies, evidenced by topographic neurotransmitter variations, give rise to the regional and systems-level dysconnectivity observed in disease.
proposals.hypothesis: The pathological progression of neurodegenerative and psychiatric diseases can be formalized as a breakdown in the hierarchical message-passing and precision-weighting within a canonical cortical microcircuit, and this local failure will predict the specific spectral and effective connectivity disturbances observed at the macroscopic scale.
proposals.method: We will construct a detailed microcircuit model of a canonical cortical column, incorporating laminar-specific cell types and receptor profiles informed by pre-2026 human and rodent data. This model will be formulated as a hierarchical generative model under the Free Energy Principle (Active Inference). Using variational message passing (e.g., belief propagation) as the inference scheme, we will simulate how specific molecular pathologies (e.g., altered NMDA or GABA-A receptor densities) perturb the system's Bayesian belief updating. The model's emergent local field potentials will be analyzed using spectral DCM to link microcircuit states to macroscopic M/EEG observables.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a mechanistic, process-based explanation for how molecular and cellular deficits translate to systems-level brain dysfunction. It will generate testable predictions for how specific receptoropathies manifest in non-invasive neuroimaging, directly informing the interpretation of studies on neurotransmitter receptor densities and spectral phenotypes in disease.
proposals.novelty: While Active Inference has been applied at regional and whole-brain scales, a rigorous application to a biologically detailed microcircuit model—explicitly linking synaptic mechanisms to Bayesian inference—remains unexplored. This moves beyond phenomenological DCM by grounding dysfunction in the failure of specific computational operations within a canonical cortical architecture.

proposals.title: A Multi-scale, Multi-modal Generative Model of Whole-Brain Dynamics: Integrating Neural Mass Oscillators with Hemodynamic and Metabolic Processes
proposals.motivation: Current whole-brain models in computational neurology often treat fMRI signals as direct proxies for neural activity, neglecting the complex, regionally heterogeneous neurovascular and neurometabolic coupling that transforms synaptic activity into the BOLD signal. This limits their biophysical interpretability and their ability to fuse multi-modal data (e.g., M/EEG and fMRI) for inferring disease mechanisms in conditions like Alzheimer's, where metabolic and vascular dysregulation are core pathophysiological features.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a generative model integrating region-specific neural mass oscillators with biophysically grounded hemodynamic and metabolic forward models will provide a more accurate and interpretable account of multi-modal neuroimaging data, enabling more precise inference on the synaptic and metabolic dysfunctions underlying regional disease progression.
proposals.method: We will extend the Dynamic Causal Modelling (DCM) framework. The neural level will comprise a network of coupled Kuramoto oscillators or neural mass models, whose synaptic outputs drive a revised Balloon-Windkessel hemodynamic model. Crucially, we will incorporate a metabolic forward model, linking neuronal energy demands (from glutamate cycling) to regional cerebral blood flow control via astrocytic signaling, parameterized using pre-2026 receptor density maps. Model inversion and parameter estimation will be performed using variational Bayesian inference (Laplace approximation) under the Free Energy principle, fitting simultaneously to empirical M/EEG spectral and fMRI time-series data.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, openly available DCM variant for truly multi-modal data fusion. It will provide a unified computational assay to disambiguate synaptic, vascular, and metabolic contributions to BOLD signals across the brain, offering a new tool for testing mechanistic hypotheses in neurodegenerative and neurovascular diseases.
proposals.novelty: While DCM exists for fMRI and neural masses separately, the explicit integration of a metabolically informed hemodynamic model within a whole-brain oscillator network, constrained by empirical neurotransmitter receptor topography, represents a significant scale-up in biophysical realism and explanatory scope for multi-modal datasets.

proposals.title: A Hierarchical Active Inference Account of Pavlovian-Instrumental Interaction: Unifying Salience, Action, and Planning
proposals.motivation: Current models of decision-making treat Pavlovian (cue-driven) and instrumental (goal-directed) systems as largely separate, yet they interact seamlessly in the healthy brain. Dysfunctions in this interaction are central to psychiatric and neurological diseases, such as addiction (excessive cue salience) and apathy in Alzheimer's disease (failure of goal-directed control). A unified computational account is needed to explain how these systems integrate to drive behaviour and how their balance breaks down in disease.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a hierarchical active inference framework can formally unify Pavlovian incentive salience and instrumental planning by casting both as minimizations of expected free energy, where Pavlovian predictions provide prior beliefs over states that bias instrumental policies.
proposals.method: We will develop a multi-level generative model where a lower 'Pavlovian' level, based on the incentive salience model, learns state-values and cue associations, generating prior preferences for a higher 'instrumental' level that engages in goal-directed planning via tree search over action sequences. The interaction is formalized through precision-weighting of messages between levels, implemented using the SPM toolbox for Dynamic Causal Modelling (DCM) and simulations. Model validation will use pre-2026 MEG/EEG and behavioural datasets to perform Bayesian model selection and compare neural signatures of inferred precision against regional neurotransmitter receptor densities.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a first-principles, process-based model of how cue-driven and goal-directed systems compete and cooperate. It will generate testable predictions for neurophysiological markers of system dominance or conflict, offering a new framework for understanding the computational pathophysiology of disorders like addiction, compulsion, and motivational deficits in dementia.
proposals.novelty: While active inference has been applied to either instrumental or Pavlovian domains separately, their formal integration within a single hierarchical architecture, with precision control mediating their interaction, is a novel synthesis. This moves beyond descriptive dual-system theories to a mechanistic, neurally plausible process model.

proposals.title: A Neurobiologically-Grounded Active Inference Account of Multiplicative Gating in Cached-Value Computations
proposals.motivation: In active inference and reinforcement learning, cached values (state-action expectations) must be contextually modulated to enable flexible behaviour. Pre-2026 theoretical work posits this as multiplicative gating, but its precise neural implementation remains unclear. Grounding this computation in specific circuits and neuromodulatory systems is essential for linking computational psychiatry to the pathophysiology of disorders like schizophrenia and addiction, where such gating is thought to be impaired.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that multiplicative gating of cached values is implemented through the convergence of glutamatergic value signals from the striatum and modulatory control signals from the prefrontal cortex onto medium spiny neurons, with gain controlled by specific neurotransmitter systems, notably acetylcholine via the basal forebrain and dopamine via nigrostriatal pathways.
proposals.method: We will construct a whole-brain dynamic causal model (DCM) for fMRI/MEG, treating striatal regions as value nodes and prefrontal regions as modulatory controllers. The model will formalize gating as a bilinear (multiplicative) coupling in the DCM state equations. We will fit this model to human neuroimaging data from a reversal learning task with pharmacological (e.g., anticholinergic) challenges. Model comparison will test if multiplicative models outperform additive alternatives, and receptor density maps (from PET atlases) will be used to constrain and validate the implicated neurotransmitter systems.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal, testable model of how a core computational operation is physically instantiated in the brain, bridging levels of analysis from molecules to circuits to computation. It will generate specific, falsifiable predictions for how distinct neuromodulators differentially sculpt value gating, offering new targets for understanding and treating neuropsychiatric diseases.
proposals.novelty: While multiplicative operations are common in abstract models, their explicit identification and formal testing within a biologically constrained, whole-brain active inference framework is novel. The integration of pharmacological manipulation with receptor-density-constrained DCM moves beyond correlation to a mechanistic, causal account.

proposals.title: Active Inference and Precision-Weighted Salience: A Generative Model of Cue Selection in Cluttered Scenes
proposals.motivation: Understanding how the brain selects specific cues for attention in cluttered environments is a core challenge in cognitive neuroscience, with direct implications for neuropsychiatric and neurodegenerative diseases where this selective process breaks down. Current computational models of visual attention, such as saliency maps, often lack a principled, neurobiologically grounded account of how top-down, contextual priors dynamically modulate the precision (salience) of specific sensory cues to guide perception and action.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that cue selection is an active inference process, where the brain resolves uncertainty by selectively increasing the precision (i.e., modulating salience) of the sensory cue that is most informative for minimizing expected free energy, given the agent's current generative model and goals.
proposals.method: We will develop a hierarchical active inference model, grounded in the Free Energy Principle, that integrates pre-2026 visual attention architectures (e.g., DeepGaze II or ICF) as part of its likelihood mapping. The model's internal states will generate predictions about scenes, and precision weighting will be dynamically applied to specific spatial features (cues) based on their expected information gain (Bayesian surprise) and goal relevance. Model inversion and belief updating will be performed using standard variational message passing, and its performance will be validated against human eye-tracking data from visual search tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal, process-level theory of attentional selection, moving beyond descriptive saliency models to a normative, Bayesian account. It will offer a novel computational framework for interpreting aberrant cue selection in conditions like schizophrenia (aberrant precision) and Alzheimer's disease, linking regional neurotransmitter receptor density variations (as per Friston's recent work) to failures in precision weighting.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in synthesizing established attention models within an active inference scheme to explain *which* cue gets selected, based on a principled (expected free energy minimizing) mechanism, rather than merely *where* attention is directed based on bottom-up features.

proposals.title: A Hierarchical Active Inference Model of State Abstraction and Value Gating in Brain Systems
proposals.motivation: Current models of brain function, particularly those based on reinforcement learning (RL), struggle to explain how high-level, slow-changing physiological states (e.g., metabolic, hormonal, or disease-related) gate low-level, cached action values. This is critical for understanding maladaptive decision-making in systemic diseases like Alzheimer's, where regional pathophysiology may alter hierarchical cognitive control. Pre-2026 work on whole-body-exposome models and receptor topography underscores the need for a formal systems-level account of how such global states constrain local neural dynamics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the brain implements a form of transfer hierarchical reinforcement learning, where a high-level 'context' or 'state' layer, informed by interoceptive and systemic signals, dynamically modulates the precision (i.e., gain) of cached value representations in lower-level sensorimotor loops, and that failures in this gating mechanism are a core feature of neurophysiological progression in brain disease.
proposals.method: We will develop a hierarchical active inference/RL model, grounded in the Free Energy Principle, with two key levels: 1) a high-level 'physiological context' model that learns abstract states from slow, systemic variables (simulating exposome/receptor density data), and 2) low-level RL policies with cached action values. The core innovation is a gating function where the high-level state outputs a precision weighting (via a softmax temperature or prior) that selectively potentiates or suppresses the low-level value estimates. We will use pre-2026 architectures like Feudal RL or Options frameworks, formalized within a Dynamic Causal Modelling (DCM) framework to fit the model to longitudinal MEG/EEG spectra from Alzheimer's cohorts.
proposals.contribution: This will provide a formal computational link between whole-body physiological states and region-specific neural computations, offering a mechanistic explanation for cognitive symptoms in systemic brain disease. It will extend hierarchical RL by incorporating transfer learning via precision-based gating, a move directly inspired by predictive coding accounts of cortical hierarchy. The resulting model will generate testable predictions for neuroimaging and inform precision medicine approaches to brain health.
proposals.novelty: While hierarchical RL exists, its integration with a transfer learning mechanism where high-level states directly gate cached values via precision—as a formal instantiation of active inference—is novel. Furthermore, applying this specifically to model the impact of slow physiological/exposome variables on fast neural dynamics bridges a key gap between systems neuroscience and whole-body medicine.

proposals.title: A Biophysical Microcircuit Model of Mesolimbic Dysfunction: Linking Spiking Neuron Dynamics to Whole-Brain Exposome Effects
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 whole-brain exposomic models, while powerful, operate at a regional or systems level, abstracting away the biophysical detail of neuronal spiking and microcircuit dynamics. This gap limits mechanistic understanding of how exposome factors (e.g., stress, toxins) alter specific neurophysiological processes in circuits central to motivation and disease, such as the mesolimbic pathway. Bridging this scale is critical for linking population-level risk to cellular-level pathophysiology in disorders like addiction and depression.
proposals.hypothesis: That the pathological effects of the exposome on mesolimbic circuit function, inferred from whole-brain models, can be mechanistically explained by specific, exposure-induced dysregulations in the excitation-inhibition balance and neuromodulatory control of a biophysically realistic spiking microcircuit.
proposals.method: We will construct a microcircuit model of the ventral tegmental area (VTA) and nucleus accumbens (NAc) using populations of adaptive exponential integrate-and-fire (AdEx) neurons with conductance-based synapses. The model will be constrained by pre-2026 data on rodent/human receptor densities (e.g., dopamine D1/D2, GABA-A) and intrinsic connectivity. We will use Dynamic Causal Modelling (DCM) for cross-scale linking: parameters of a canonical microcircuit DCM, fitted to empirical M/EEG from exposomic cohorts, will be used to define the perturbation regimes (e.g., altered synaptic gains, neuromodulatory tone) for the spiking model. The model's output will be analysed using spectral and information-theoretic measures to compare with intracranial EEG findings.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a first-principles, biophysical account of how macroscopic exposome signals manifest in microcircuit spiking dynamics, directly linking Friston's active inference framework to cellular neurophysiology. It will generate testable predictions about which specific ion channel or synaptic mechanisms are most vulnerable to exposome factors, offering new targets for precision intervention in circuit-based psychiatry.
proposals.novelty: While microcircuit models and whole-brain exposomic models exist separately, this proposal uniquely uses DCM as a formal bridge to condition a biophysical spiking model on population-level, model-based inferences from exposome data, moving beyond correlation to a mechanistic, cross-scale explanation.

proposals.title: Inverting the Generative Model of Incentive Salience: A Variational Bayesian Framework for Inferring Hidden Physiological States from Cue-Triggered 'Wanting'
proposals.motivation: A core challenge in computational psychiatry and neurology is the 'inverse problem' of inferring hidden pathophysiological states from observable behavior. Pre-2026, Friston's active inference framework provides a principled account of how internal states generate behavior, but the reverse mapping—deducing state from patterned behavioral responses—remains under-specified. This is critical for disorders like addiction and apathy syndromes, where aberrant cue-triggered 'wanting' (a quantifiable behavioral pattern) signals specific dysregulations in mesocorticolimbic circuits and interoceptive systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an agent's pattern of cue-trigved 'wanting' behavior is a sufficient statistic to uniquely infer its latent physiological and belief states, formalized within a hierarchical active inference model, and that these inferred states will map onto specific neurochemical receptor density profiles and disease trajectories.
proposals.method: We will construct a generative model of incentive salience, where 'wanting' behaviors (e.g., vigor, latency, choice) are emitted by an agent whose dynamics follow the Free Energy Principle. The inverse problem will be solved using variational Bayesian inversion (e.g., variational Laplace) of this model given observed behavioral time-series. We will ground the model's hidden states (e.g., precision weights, expected free energy) in pre-2026 neurobiological evidence, linking them to regional neurotransmitter receptor densities (e.g., from JuSpace) and whole-body exposome models to constrain the inversion. The inversion scheme will be validated using synthetic data and applied to longitudinal magnetoencephalography (MEG) and behavioral data from prodromal Alzheimer's and addiction cohorts.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a formal, testable method for non-invasive 'computational phenotyping' of hidden disease states from simple behavioral assays. It will provide a mechanistic bridge between Friston's systems-level active inference theory and regional neurophysiological progression models, offering a new tool for early detection and subtyping of brain disorders based on computational behavior analysis.
proposals.novelty: While active inference describes how states cause behavior, this project inverts that causality to infer states from behavior, operationalizing a true 'computational biomarker'. It uniquely integrates the cognitive neuroscience of 'wanting' with whole-body exposome and receptor topography models within a single variational inversion framework.

proposals.title: Active Inference in Continuous Time: A Generative Model for Dynamic Motivational Modulation in Real-World Robotic Tasks
proposals.motivation: Current active inference implementations for agents often operate in discrete time, limiting their applicability to real-world, continuous-time interactions where motivational states (e.g., hunger, curiosity) must be dynamically modulated based on changing bodily and environmental states. Grounding such models in continuous time is essential for scaling from theoretical neuroscience to embodied AI and robotics, offering a principled path to explain how regional brain dynamics support adaptive behavior in complex, open-ended tasks.
proposals.hypothesis: Formulating active inference and its associated message passing (e.g., variational filtering) in continuous time will enable an agent to dynamically modulate its prior preferences (i.e., motivational states) and precision weights, leading to more robust and adaptive goal-directed behavior in physically realistic simulations compared to discrete-time approximations.
proposals.method: We will extend the generalized coordinates of motion framework for variational filtering to a continuous-time state-space model, where the agent's generative model includes hierarchically coupled dynamics for hidden states of the world and its own interoceptive (bodily) states. The agent's prior preferences will be formulated as time-varying attractors, with their salience (expected precision) controlled by a slow, meta-level dynamic driven by free energy gradients. This model will be implemented and tested in a PyBullet or MuJoCo simulation of a robotic agent performing a prolonged foraging task requiring dynamic switching between exploration and energy-replenishment behaviors.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal, neurobiologically plausible bridge between continuous-time theories of brain dynamics (e.g., Dynamic Causal Modelling) and embodied AI. It will yield a publicly available simulation framework for testing active inference in continuous domains and generate specific predictions about how failure in motivational modulation dynamics could manifest in aberrant behavior, linking to computational psychiatry.
proposals.novelty: While continuous-time formulations of predictive coding exist, their integration into a full active inference scheme with dynamically modulated interoceptive priors for real-world robotic control is novel. This moves beyond applying discrete cognitive architectures to robotics by directly implementing the continuous dynamics inherent to biological systems.

proposals.title: Nested Markov Blankets: A Hierarchical Active Inference Model of Endogenous Sensory and Active State Dynamics
proposals.motivation: The free energy principle (FEP) and active inference treat sensory and active states as a Markov blanket, mediating inference but often modelled as simple, direct mappings. In complex systems like the brain, these blanket states (e.g., sensory receptor activations, proprioceptive signals, and preparatory motor commands) themselves exhibit rich, context-sensitive dynamics shaped by internal neuromodulatory and cognitive control systems. Ignoring this internal complexity limits the explanatory power of active inference for multi-scale neurocognitive phenomena and whole-body-exposome interactions in health and disease.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that endowing sensory and active blanket states with their own internal, inferential dynamics—formalized as nested Markov blankets within a hierarchical generative model—will provide a more accurate and powerful account of adaptive perception and action, particularly in explaining regional neurophysiological progression in disorders like Alzheimer's disease.
proposals.method: We will develop a hierarchical active inference scheme where each level's blanket states (sensory and active) are themselves the internal states of a subordinate Markov blanket. This requires extending the state-space and introducing precision parameters that are dynamically modulated by the superordinate level's beliefs (cognitive control). We will implement this using dynamic causal modelling (DCM) for time-series data (e.g., M/EEG) and apply it to existing longitudinal magnetoencephalography datasets from Alzheimer's cohorts, testing for altered hierarchical message-passing between nested blankets as a marker of disease progression.
proposals.contribution: This work will formally unify the FEP with multi-scale systems neuroscience, providing a novel computational architecture for understanding how cognitive control and evidence accumulation emerge from the interaction of nested inference loops. It will yield new, testable models of how disruptions in these hierarchical dynamics manifest as specific spectral and connectivity signatures in brain disease.
proposals.novelty: While Markov blankets are central to the FEP, existing formulations treat them as static interfaces; this proposal fundamentally reconceptualizes them as active, inferential subsystems with their own dynamics, creating a truly recursive and scale-free model of embodied cognition.

proposals.title: Active Inference of Hidden States: A Dual-Particle Model for External Observer Dynamics in Brain Disease
proposals.motivation: In computational psychiatry and neurology, a core challenge is inferring a patient's hidden pathophysiological states from external observations (e.g., behavior, M/EEG). This mirrors the 'dual problem' in physics, where an observer infers the internal state of a 'strange' system. Grounded in Friston's recent work on whole-body-exposome models and neurophysiological progression in Alzheimer's, this proposal formalizes the observer as an active inference agent, creating a unified framework for biomarker discovery and clinical inference.
proposals.hypothesis: The process by which an external observer (e.g., a clinician or diagnostic algorithm) optimally infers the hidden internal states of a 'strange particle' (e.g., a diseased brain region) can be formally described as active inference, where the observer's generative model is hierarchically coupled to the particle's generative model of its own niche.
proposals.method: We will construct a hierarchical active inference model where the 'particle' is a simulated neural system (e.g., a canonical microcircuit with regional receptor density variations) undergoing a disease process (e.g., synaptic loss). The 'observer' is a separate active inference agent that receives only the particle's observable outputs (spectral features). Using Dynamic Causal Modelling (DCM) for inference and Bayesian model reduction for learning, we will simulate how the observer's prior beliefs (shaped by population-level 'exposome' models) constrain and accelerate the identification of the particle's hidden states (e.g., synaptic gain). The observer's actions will be interventions (e.g., simulated pharmacological perturbations) to disambiguate states.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal, testable model for clinical reasoning and biomarker interpretation, linking individual-level neurophysiological progression to population-level exposome data. It will yield novel metrics for quantifying the 'inferability' of pathological states, which could guide the design of diagnostic protocols and explain differences in diagnostic accuracy across diseases and observers.
proposals.novelty: While active inference typically models a single agent, the novelty lies in explicitly modeling the *duality* of two coupled active inference systems (patient and observer), applying this to the concrete problem of inferring disease states from neurophysiological data, where the observer's model is informed by pre-2026 topographic receptor maps and whole-body exposome constraints.

proposals.title: A Variational Bayesian Framework for Structured Dynamic Motif Decomposition of Non-Stationary Neurophysiological Signals
proposals.motivation: Current analyses of within-trial neural dynamics, such as those in M/EEG, often treat non-stationarity as unstructured noise or rely on overly flexible models that obscure interpretability. This limits our ability to identify the fundamental, reproducible building blocks of neural computation that are engaged across tasks and perturbed in disease. A principled method to extract a shared dictionary of prototypical dynamic motifs is crucial for linking specific neural dynamic patterns to cognitive processes and their pathological alterations.
proposals.hypothesis: That the observed non-stationary spectral dynamics in neurophysiological data can be parsimoniously explained by a sparse, linear combination of a small set of smooth, prototypical temporal motifs, which are latent variables shared across trials, conditions, and individuals.
proposals.method: We will develop a hierarchical, generative model within the variational Bayesian (Free Energy) framework. The model posits that observed time-frequency data is generated from a dictionary of smooth, parameterized temporal basis functions (motifs). Inference will employ stochastic variational inference to learn both the shared motif dictionary and the trial-specific sparse activation weights. Model comparison and complexity penalties will be handled via Bayesian model reduction and Free Energy minimization, ensuring robust separation of signal from noise.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, interpretable tool for characterizing the dynamic repertoire of brain systems. It will enable direct hypothesis testing about which motifs are recruited by specific cognitive states or clinical conditions, moving beyond descriptive power changes. The method will be validated on existing MEG datasets from healthy aging and Alzheimer's disease cohorts to demonstrate its utility for precision neuroscience.
proposals.novelty: Unlike standard non-stationary models or time-frequency decompositions, this approach explicitly models the *structured forms* of dynamics as shared latent variables, providing a generative, low-dimensional, and causally interpretable account of neural transients that generalizes across trials and conditions.

proposals.title: A Hierarchical Bayesian Model for Disentangling Neural Dynamics and Autocorrelated Noise in Time-Frequency Power: A Free Energy and Dynamic Causal Modelling Approach
proposals.motivation: Current analyses of time-frequency power in M/EEG often conflate structured neural dynamics with session-specific, autocorrelated measurement noise, leading to confounded inferences about brain function in health and disease. This is a critical barrier for precision models of brain health and for understanding neurophysiological progression in conditions like Alzheimer's disease, where separating signal from noise is paramount. A principled, model-based separation is needed to ground findings in robust neural mechanisms rather than artefactual dependencies.
proposals.hypothesis: That the dependency structure in time-frequency power can be formally separated into a sparse, smooth component explainable by hierarchical neural dynamics (state dynamics) and a generic, session-specific autocorrelated noise process, and that the inferred neural component will provide a more robust and interpretable link to cognitive and behavioural variables.
proposals.method: We will develop a hierarchical Bayesian model within the Active Inference/Free Energy Principle framework. The neural dynamics layer will be modelled using a sparse, temporally smooth prior informed by canonical microcircuit models and topographic receptor density maps. The noise process will be modelled as a session-specific autoregressive (AR) process. Variational Bayesian inversion (e.g., variational Laplace) will be used for model estimation. The model's validity will be tested using predictive accuracy on held-out data and its correlation with behavioural measures, compared to standard methods.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, validated tool for decomposing time-frequency data, directly enhancing the interpretability of M/EEG studies in cognitive neuroscience and clinical populations. It will contribute a formal, generative model-based approach to the 'exposome' of neural signals, separating endogenous brain dynamics from exogenous measurement confounds. The output will be a robust metric of neural dependency for use in disease progression models and brain-state decoding.
proposals.novelty: While AR models for noise exist, their integration within a hierarchical Bayesian model that explicitly uses neurophysiologically informed priors to constrain the *neural* dependencies represents a significant advance. This moves beyond descriptive separation to a generative model that embodies a causal hypothesis about the layered sources of observed spectral power.

proposals.title: Kriging the Neural Manifold: A Variographic Framework for Denoising and Inference on Non-independent Time-Frequency Power
proposals.motivation: Current statistical models for M/EEG time-frequency power often assume independence across time, frequency, and trials, violating the intrinsic smoothness and autocorrelation of neural dynamics. This leads to inefficient estimation, inflated false positives in group-level inference, and a loss of sensitivity to structured, low-SNR signals. A principled model of these dependencies is crucial for robust biomarker discovery in neurological and psychiatric diseases.
proposals.hypothesis: Neural oscillatory power is a spatially autocorrelated process within a multi-dimensional manifold defined by time, frequency, and trial dimensions, and modeling this covariance structure with geostatistical tools will provide more efficient, unbiased estimates and superior statistical inference compared to independence-assuming methods.
proposals.method: We will formalize the time-frequency-trial space as a 3D manifold with a custom distance metric. Empirical variograms will be constructed from single-subject MEG data (e.g., from the aforementioned AD study) to estimate the covariance structure. This model will be integrated into a kriging (Gaussian process regression) framework to generate best linear unbiased predictors (BLUPs) of denoised power. The approach will be validated within a Parametric Empirical Bayes (PEB) or Dynamic Causal Modelling (DCM) framework for group-level and network-level inference, respectively.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous statistical tool for denoising neural power estimates and improving the sensitivity of disease progression models. It directly contributes to Friston's active inference agenda by offering a better model of the likelihood (observation model) for oscillatory data, enhancing the evidence for model comparison in studies of brain disease.
proposals.novelty: While spatial smoothing is common in neuroimaging, explicitly treating the time-frequency plane as a geostatistical manifold to model and exploit its intrinsic covariance for optimal estimation is a novel synthesis of spatial ecology and computational neuroscience.

proposals.title: A Hierarchical Bayesian Framework for Valid Inference on Neural Phase-Locking in Health and Disease
proposals.motivation: Phase-locking measures like PLV are fundamental for quantifying functional connectivity in M/EEG, yet standard GLM-based inference on these bounded, non-Gaussian variables is statistically invalid. This mismatch undermines the reliability of findings in studies of cognitive systems and disease progression, such as Alzheimer's, where subtle changes in synchronization are critical biomarkers. A principled statistical framework is therefore essential for robustly testing hypotheses about brain network dynamics.
proposals.hypothesis: That a hierarchical Bayesian model, employing appropriate likelihoods (e.g., Beta for PLV, von Mises for phase angles) and accounting for the nested structure of neuroimaging data, will provide statistically valid and more sensitive inference on phase-locking compared to conventional linear models, revealing previously obscured disease and cognitive state effects.
proposals.method: We will develop a generalized linear mixed model (GLMM) where the conditional distribution of phase-locking values is specified by a Beta likelihood with a logit link, and phase angles by a von Mises likelihood. Hierarchical random effects will model subject- and region-specific variations. Inference will be performed using Variational Bayesian or Hamiltonian Monte Carlo methods, with model validation via parametric bootstrapping and posterior predictive checks. The framework will be integrated with Dynamic Causal Modelling to relate synaptic parameters to phase-locking.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a statistically rigorous tool for the neuroimaging community, ensuring false-positive rates are controlled in connectivity studies. It will directly advance the analysis of longitudinal MEG data in Alzheimer's disease and enable more precise mapping of cognitive control mechanisms onto phase-synchronized networks.
proposals.novelty: While Beta regression exists, its integration into a hierarchical Bayesian framework for neuroimaging, combined with validation through generative (predictive) model checking, represents a novel synthesis of statistical rigour and generative modelling principles central to active inference.

proposals.title: Inverting the Hierarchy: Normative Transformations as Reflexive Primitives in Hierarchical Predictive Coding
proposals.motivation: Predictive coding posits that the brain's primary function is to minimize prediction error. However, this framework often assumes complex, high-level generative models as the starting point, leaving the origins of the most basic sensory regularities unexplained. This project addresses a foundational gap: whether the canonical nonlinear transformations (e.g., log, sigmoid) used to normalize neural data are merely analytical tools or reflect hardwired, reflexive operations that form the computational bedrock for more elaborate predictions.
proposals.hypothesis: That normative data transformations (e.g., logarithmic warping) empirically outperform linear mappings when embedded as the first-stage 'kernel' in a hierarchical model of sensory processing, and that higher-level predictive tuning is best modelled as an elaboration of these reflexive normalization primitives rather than assuming prediction as the primary goal.
proposals.method: We will employ Dynamic Causal Modelling (DCM) for electrophysiological data (M/EEG), constructing hierarchical models where the first-level forward mapping is parameterized by different normative transformations (log, Box-Cox, softplus). Using Bayesian model inversion and comparison (Bayesian Model Reduction, Variational Laplace), we will fit these models to neural responses from paradigms manipulating stimulus predictability (e.g., oddball, mismatch negativity). Model evidence will be compared against standard linear-nonlinear and pure predictive coding architectures to determine which primitive best explains the data across cortical hierarchies.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, empirically grounded link between low-level sensory physiology and high-level Bayesian theories of the brain. It will shift the explanatory focus in predictive coding from 'top-down predictions' to 'bottom-up reflexive kernels,' offering a new perspective on the computational architecture of sensory systems. The findings could redefine normative mappings from mere analysis steps to candidate biological operations, impacting models of perception and disease progression where such transformations may break down.
proposals.novelty: It inverts the standard modeling primacy in predictive coding by treating reflexive normalization as the fundamental primitive, rather than a consequence of prediction. This approach systematically tests whether well-known statistical transformations from data science are neurally instantiated as fixed computational kernels.

proposals.title: A Variational Bayesian Framework for Joint Source Reconstruction and Parametric Oscillation Modelling in MEG/EEG
proposals.motivation: Current MEG/EEG analysis pipelines separate source localization and spectral characterization, introducing errors as spatial leakage from distributed source solutions corrupts estimated oscillation parameters. This confounds the study of regional brain dynamics in health and disease, particularly for investigations of oscillatory dysregulation in conditions like Alzheimer's disease. A unified estimation framework is needed to provide oscillation parameters with uncertainty estimates that are intrinsically corrected for the fundamental ambiguity of the electromagnetic inverse problem.
proposals.hypothesis: By inverting a single generative model that explicitly parameterizes source-level activity as a mixture of oscillatory processes, we can simultaneously and more accurately estimate both the spatial origin and the spectrotemporal characteristics of neural oscillations, with formal uncertainty quantification.
proposals.method: We will develop a hierarchical generative model where latent cortical source activity is defined by a parametric oscillation model (e.g., a mixture of damped oscillators or stochastic nonlinear oscillators with regionally-varying parameters). This source model is projected via the lead-field to generate sensor data. Model inversion will be performed using Variational Laplace (a core Free Energy/Active Inference algorithm), enabling efficient Bayesian estimation of the joint posterior over source locations and oscillation parameters (frequency, damping, power). Priors will incorporate pre-2026 knowledge, such as empirical neurotransmitter receptor density maps, to inform regional variation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel tool for precision neuroimaging that yields spectrotemporal parameters with credible intervals accounting for source leakage. It will advance the neurophysiological characterization of disease progression by enabling more reliable inference of region-specific oscillatory abnormalities. The framework formally integrates generative models of dynamics with the biophysics of signal propagation.
proposals.novelty: Unlike existing two-step methods or distributed source solutions followed by spectral analysis, this approach performs a single Bayesian inversion for both space and spectrum, directly estimating oscillation parameters from sensors while marginalizing over source location uncertainty.

proposals.title: Inferring the Evolutionary Trajectory of Cortical Dynamics: A Phylogenetic Comparative Analysis of Source-Reconstructed Oscillatory Phenotypes
proposals.motivation: Understanding the evolution of the human brain has focused on anatomy and connectivity, leaving the evolution of its fundamental dynamic processes—cortical oscillations—largely unexplored. This gap limits our ability to formulate principled hypotheses about how selective pressures shaped the neurodynamic architectures underlying cognition and their vulnerability in neuropsychiatric disease. Integrating evolutionary biology with systems neuroscience provides a novel framework to move beyond static comparisons.
proposals.hypothesis: The parameters governing cortical oscillations (e.g., alpha peak frequency, beta/gamma coupling) evolve in a manner consistent with phylogenetic history and are correlated with species-specific cognitive and behavioral traits, allowing for reliable statistical reconstruction of ancestral neurodynamic states.
proposals.method: We will apply source-localization (e.g., beamforming) to resting-state MEG/EEG data from extant primate and mammalian species to extract region-specific oscillatory phenotypes. Using Bayesian phylogenetic comparative methods (e.g., Brownian motion, Ornstein-Uhlenbeck models implemented in software like `phytools` or `RevBayes`), we will infer the evolutionary history of these dynamic traits. The analysis will control for allometry and leverage established species phylogenies to test for correlated evolution between oscillatory parameters and behavioral proxies.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first quantitative timeline of ancestral cortical dynamics, offering a new lens on brain evolution. It will generate testable predictions about the neurodynamic foundations of cognitive specialization and provide an evolutionary baseline for understanding deviations in oscillatory phenotypes associated with brain disease.
proposals.novelty: While phylogenetic methods have been used for neuroanatomy, their application to *in vivo* neurophysiological time-series data to reconstruct ancestral brain dynamics is unprecedented. It shifts the evolutionary focus from structure to the intrinsic dynamics of functional networks.

proposals.title: A Bayesian State-Space Framework for Neural Oscillatory Dynamics: An Ecological Analogy for Robust Inference
proposals.motivation: Current analyses of neural oscillatory power (e.g., from M/EEG) predominantly rely on frequentist General Linear Models (GLMs) applied to log-transformed power. This approach struggles with the inherently non-normal, non-negative nature of power data and conflates measurement noise with the latent neural dynamics of interest. A principled statistical framework is needed to separate these processes and provide full uncertainty quantification for parameters governing neural responses in health and disease.
proposals.hypothesis: Neural oscillatory power dynamics can be more accurately modeled as a latent stochastic process, analogous to population dynamics in ecology, where a Gamma or log-Normal state-space model explicitly separates the evolution of the neural state from observation noise, yielding superior parameter estimates and uncertainty quantification compared to standard methods.
proposals.method: We will adapt hierarchical Bayesian state-space models from ecological time-series analysis. The latent neural state will evolve according to a Gamma or log-Normal process model, with its parameters structured as a GLM incorporating experimental variables. Observation models will account for the statistical properties of power estimates (e.g., using a Gamma likelihood). Inference will be performed using Hamiltonian Monte Carlo (HMC) via Stan or similar probabilistic programming languages, enabling full posterior estimation of latent states, GLM parameters, and hyperparameters.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a robust, statistically coherent alternative to frequentist GLMs for oscillatory power, directly modeling its non-normal distribution. It will offer the field a tool for distinguishing true neural dynamics from measurement error and for quantifying uncertainty in the effects of experimental manipulations or disease states on neural oscillations, directly supporting predictive coding and active inference accounts of brain function.
