proposals.novelty: While state-space models exist in neuroscience (e.g., for firing rates), their application to oscillatory power via a direct analogy to ecological population models is novel. The explicit use of non-normal distributions for both state and observation processes within a fully Bayesian GLM framework for M/EEG power represents a significant methodological advance over standard practices.

proposals.title: A State-Space Model of Neural Excitability: Tracking Latent Volatility in Oscillatory Power Using Dynamic Causal Frameworks
proposals.motivation: Current analyses of neural oscillatory power often treat trial-by-trial fluctuations as independent noise, obscuring the dynamics of intrinsic neural excitability that may be fundamental to cognition and disease. In financial econometrics, sophisticated state-space models successfully separate latent volatility from noisy returns, providing a principled analogy for neuroscience. Applying this to neural data could reveal how regional excitability states evolve during tasks and how their dysregulation contributes to neuropsychiatric and neurodegenerative disorders.
proposals.hypothesis: That trial-by-trial fluctuations in measured band-limited power are driven by a latent, dynamically evolving 'neural volatility' state, which can be formally estimated and which shows characteristic, systems-level adaptations during cognitive tasks and pathological progression in diseases like Alzheimer's.
proposals.method: We will develop a hierarchical Bayesian model where observed oscillatory power (e.g., from M/EEG) is the observed variable in a Generalized Linear Model (GLM). The GLM's trial-wise scaling parameter will be governed by a latent state variable, modeled as a stochastic process estimated via a Kalman filter or particle filter, directly analogous to stochastic volatility models in finance. This model will be integrated into a Dynamic Causal Modelling (DCM) framework to infer how this latent volatility is controlled by extrinsic inputs and intrinsic connections within a defined network.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel quantitative assay for time-varying neural excitability, moving beyond descriptive power measures. It will offer a new model-based biomarker for tracking pathological progression in longitudinal studies of brain disease. Furthermore, it formalizes a link between computational principles in econometrics and systems neuroscience, enriching both fields.
proposals.novelty: While state-space models exist in neuroscience, the direct translation of financial volatility-tracking algorithms to model neural power dynamics is novel. This approach explicitly treats excitability as a latent stochastic process to be filtered from noise, a significant conceptual and technical advance over standard trial-averaging or windowed variance techniques.

proposals.title: A Variational Free-Energy Framework for Conditional Generative Inference of Single-Trial Neural Oscillations
proposals.motivation: Current analyses of neural oscillations rely on averaging across trials, discarding trial-specific dynamics crucial for understanding cognitive processes and their disintegration in disease. Single-trial inference is statistically ill-posed, preventing the characterization of oscillatory power and its uncertainty for individual events. This gap limits our ability to link momentary neural states to behaviour or pathology in frameworks like predictive coding.
proposals.hypothesis: The free-energy principle provides a normative foundation for treating single-trial spectral inference as Bayesian model inversion, where a deep generative model, trained on population data, can furnish a rigorous posterior over oscillatory features for a single observation.
proposals.method: We will develop a conditional Hierarchical Variational Autoencoder (cHVAE) architecture. The encoder will map high-dimensional time-series data (e.g., M/EEG) and trial-conditioning variables (e.g., stimulus, behaviour) to a latent space. The decoder will generate synthetic time-series and their associated power spectra. Training will minimize a free-energy bound (ELBO) on a large corpus of multi-trial data. For a new single trial, we will perform amortized variational inference using the trained encoder to obtain a full posterior distribution over latent oscillatory power, conditioned on the trial's features.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first principled, uncertainty-quantified method for spectral analysis of individual trials, directly operationalizing the free-energy principle for generative inference. It will enable new tests of predictive coding theories at the single-event level and offer a novel biomarker for trial-by-trial neurophysiological progression in disorders like Alzheimer's disease.
proposals.novelty: While generative models exist, their application as a *statistical engine* for inverting single-trial observations into a full posterior over spectral features—grounded in variational free-energy—is a novel synthesis of deep learning and theoretical neurobiology.

proposals.title: A Generative Bayesian Framework for Decoding Sensory Stimuli from Oscillatory Power Manifolds in Whole-Brain Exposome Models
proposals.motivation: Current decoding models, like reverse GLMs, treat stimulus-to-neural mappings as deterministic, failing to capture the inherent ambiguity and one-to-many nature of neural representations. This is a critical limitation for understanding brain function in naturalistic settings and for developing robust neuroprosthetics. A generative approach is needed to quantify the full probabilistic relationship between complex stimuli and distributed neural dynamics, particularly oscillatory power, which is central to cognitive systems and altered in disease.
proposals.hypothesis: That a generative hierarchical model, employing active inference and equipped with strong empirical priors on stimulus properties, can learn a latent manifold that accurately captures the probabilistic mapping from oscillatory power features to a distribution over plausible stimuli, outperforming deterministic decoders in accuracy and providing confidence estimates.
proposals.method: We will construct a hierarchical generative model within the active inference (Free Energy Principle) framework. The model will treat latent stimulus properties as hidden causes and observed spectral power features (e.g., from M/EEG) as sensory data. We will use Variational Laplace for model inversion and learning, incorporating empirically derived priors (e.g., from neurotransmitter receptor topography) to constrain the stimulus manifold. The decoder will output a full posterior distribution over the stimulus space, with confidence quantified by posterior precision.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, probabilistic tool for decoding ambiguous stimuli, directly applicable to probing neural representations in health and their disintegration in diseases like Alzheimer's. It will advance the field by moving from point estimates to full Bayesian belief updating, offering a novel link between generative models of brain function and practical decoding for precision brain health applications.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying the active inference framework—a process theory for brain function—to the specific inverse problem of stimulus decoding, moving beyond descriptive machine learning to a biologically grounded generative model that explicitly solves the one-to-many mapping via Bayesian inversion.

proposals.title: Beyond Inference: A Predictive Coding Account of Reflexive Dysregulation in Functional Neurological Disorders
proposals.motivation: The dominant free-energy principle (FEP) framework posits the brain as a purely inferential, prediction-error-minimizing organ. However, this struggles to fully explain acute, stimulus-bound symptoms in functional neurological disorders (FND), such as non-epileptic seizures or functional movement disorders, which appear reflexive and non-inferential. Reconciling these phenomena with the FEP is crucial for a unified theory of brain function and pathology.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that certain psychopathologies, exemplified by FND, emerge not from faulty inference per se, but from a pathological collapse of hierarchical generative models, causing a reversion to low-level, reflexive (i.e., 'habitual') control systems that bypass contextual, inferential processing.
proposals.method: We will construct a hierarchical dynamic causal model (DCM) of sensorimotor loops, incorporating both inferential (state) and reflexive (control) states. Using Bayesian model reduction, we will fit this model to pre-existing MEG/EEG data from FND patients and controls during a mismatch negativity paradigm and a startle reflex task. We will quantify the evidence for a shift in the balance of model evidence towards simpler, reflexive models in patients, and link these parameters to regional neurotransmitter receptor density maps (e.g., GABA-A) to ground the dysfunction in specific neurophysiological substrates.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal, model-based account of how 'non-inferential' symptoms can arise within a predictive coding architecture, bridging a critical gap between theoretical neuroscience and clinical phenomenology. It will yield testable biomarkers (DCM parameters) for a class of disorders often considered medically unexplained, moving towards a precision psychiatry framework for FND.
proposals.novelty: While Friston's work has extensively modeled inference and belief updating, this proposal explicitly models the failure of inference and the conditions under which the brain defaults to a reflexive mode, challenging the view of the brain as *purely* inferential.

proposals.title: Beyond Generative Failure: A Systems Control Theory of Symptom Emergence in Functional Neurological and Dissociative Disorders
proposals.motivation: The free-energy principle and active inference provide a powerful framework for understanding perception and action as approximate Bayesian inference. However, its central focus on generative model failures (i.e., inaccurate priors or likelihoods) may not fully capture the pathophysiology of certain psychiatric disorders. Pre-2026, disorders like Functional Neurological Disorder (FND) and Dissociative Identity Disorder (DID) present symptoms (e.g., paralysis, identity fragmentation) that appear to arise not from faulty inference per se, but from failures in higher-order control systems that govern the deployment and integration of latent states and sensory evidence.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that core symptoms in FND and related dissociative disorders emerge from a dysregulation in the hierarchical control of precision weighting (i.e., expected reliability) and state-switching, a failure situated in the meta-control of inference rather than in the generative models themselves.
proposals.method: We will construct a multi-scale, hierarchical active inference architecture, explicitly separating generative models (for sensation and interoception) from a meta-control layer governing state transitions and precision allocation. Using synthetic agents, we will simulate symptom emergence by lesioning specific control-theoretic components (e.g., destabilizing the transition model in the control layer) while preserving intact generative models. We will fit this framework to pre-existing multimodal neuroimaging (fMRI/MEG) and behavioral data from FND patients using Bayesian model reduction and Dynamic Causal Modelling to identify disorder-specific dysregulations in the control hierarchy.
proposals.contribution: This work will formally extend active inference theory by delineating a theory of 'control dysregulation' distinct from 'model corruption.' It will provide a novel computational phenotype for FND and dissociative disorders, moving beyond descriptive symptom checklists. The resulting framework will offer new targets for neuromodulation and psychotherapy aimed at restoring control stability.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in formally decoupling the failure of control systems from the integrity of generative models within the active inference framework, challenging the assumption that all psychopathology stems from suboptimal Bayesian inference. It applies systems control theory to the specific problem of symptomogenesis in disorders characterized by paradoxical willed action.

proposals.title: A Generative Model of Belief-Induced Pathophysiology: Inverting the Causal Arrow in Psychosis Using Active Inference
proposals.motivation: Current models of psychiatric disorders like schizophrenia primarily frame pathophysiology (e.g., synaptic dysconnectivity) as the cause of symptoms (e.g., delusions). This unidirectional view neglects the possibility that sustained, aberrant perceptual beliefs, once established, could themselves drive maladaptive synaptic and circuit-level changes through Hebbian and homeostatic plasticity. Resolving this causal direction is critical for understanding disease progression and treatment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that sustained, high-precision (low uncertainty) aberrant beliefs, formalized within an active inference framework, will induce synaptic and regional dysconnectivity consistent with observed pathophysiology in early psychosis, through simulated neurophysiological processes of long-term potentiation and depression.
proposals.method: We will construct a hierarchical dynamic causal model (DCM) of a cortical hierarchy (e.g., auditory/visual to prefrontal). The model will incorporate synaptic plasticity rules (e.g., spike-timing-dependent plasticity) at the connection level. Using simulated M/EEG data, we will invert the model under two regimes: first, with fixed pathological connectivity generating aberrant beliefs, and second, with initially normal connectivity but where sustained aberrant beliefs (encoded as precise prior expectations) drive the plasticity rules to alter connectivity. Bayesian model comparison will assess which causal direction provides a better account of empirical, longitudinal MEG data from clinical high-risk individuals.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal, computational proof-of-concept for a top-down, belief-driven pathophysiology, challenging purely bottom-up disease models. It will extend active inference from a descriptive framework to a generative model of disease progression, offering novel targets for cognitive therapies aimed at belief precision.
proposals.novelty: While DCM and active inference have been used to infer pathophysiology from symptoms, inverting this to show how symptoms *cause* pathophysiology via explicit plasticity mechanisms is a novel, non-obvious application of the framework, moving from correlation to a testable model of circular causality.

proposals.title: Beyond Precision: A Dynamical Systems and Information-Geometric Account of Uncertainty Encoding in Regional Brain Pathologies
proposals.motivation: The Free Energy Principle (FEP) and active inference formalise uncertainty exclusively through Bayesian precision, a commitment that may be overly restrictive. Pre-2026 evidence, including regional neurotransmitter receptor maps and whole-body exposome models, suggests brain disease involves complex, non-Bayesian uncertainty dynamics not fully captured by precision-weighted prediction errors. This project addresses a critical gap in theoretical neuroscience by exploring alternative mathematical descriptions of uncertainty.
proposals.hypothesis: Neural systems, particularly in disease states like Alzheimer's, employ uncertainty representations that are more parsimoniously described by formalisms from dynamical systems theory (e.g., local Lyapunov exponents) and information geometry (e.g., Fisher information metrics) than by Bayesian precision alone.
proposals.method: We will construct generative models of regional neurophysiology (e.g., MEG/EEG spectra) using Dynamic Causal Modelling (DCM). Instead of solely inverting these models under the Laplace approximation (inherently Bayesian), we will apply tools from control theory and non-equilibrium thermodynamics to analyse their dynamics. Specifically, we will quantify uncertainty via the stability manifolds of system attractors and the curvature of information manifolds derived from receptor density topographies, comparing these measures to classical precision estimates in healthy and diseased cohorts.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel mathematical toolkit for characterising uncertainty in brain systems, directly linking regional pathophysiology to formal measures of dynamical instability and information loss. It will challenge and potentially expand the FEP, offering new targets for model-based disease stratification and a principled way to integrate multi-scale exposome data into brain health models.
proposals.novelty: While previous work uses Bayesian precision within the FEP, this proposal explicitly relaxes that commitment to test if alternative, non-probabilistic formalisms offer a superior account of uncertainty in empirical neuropathological data.

proposals.title: Validating Whole-Body Exposome Models of Brain Disease: A Causal Discovery Framework for Pathophysiological Pathways
proposals.motivation: Current generative models of brain disease, such as whole-body-exposome frameworks, propose complex, multi-scale causal pathways linking systemic factors to regional brain pathophysiology. However, validating these model-predicted pathways against real-world, multimodal data remains a critical challenge. Pre-2026, there is a gap between the rich causal hypotheses generated by these models and the application of formal, data-driven causal discovery methods to test them.
proposals.hypothesis: That causal discovery algorithms, applied to multimodal neuroimaging, exposomic, and clinical data, will recover directed graphical structures that are consistent with, and can therefore validate, the key pathophysiological pathways posited by generative whole-body models of diseases like Alzheimer's.
proposals.method: We will integrate high-dimensional multimodal data (e.g., M/EEG, receptor density maps, blood biomarkers, environmental exposures) from longitudinal cohorts. Using a two-stage approach, we will first apply constraint-based causal discovery algorithms (e.g., PC or FCI algorithms) and score-based methods (using Bayesian Information Criterion scores) to learn a data-driven causal Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG). This DAG will then be formally compared, using graph similarity metrics and d-separation tests, to the causal structure inherent in a pre-specified generative model (e.g., a Dynamic Causal Model or active inference scheme) of disease progression.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal, empirical framework for validating complex systems-level models of brain disease, moving beyond correlational fits. It will demonstrate how causal inference can bridge generative modelling and observational data, offering a principled method to refine model architectures and identify the most critical, data-supported pathways for therapeutic intervention.
proposals.novelty: While causal discovery and generative modelling are both established, their direct integration for model validation is novel. This approach uses data-driven causal graphs not as the final product, but as an independent empirical test for the architectural assumptions of first-principles models of whole-brain-body disease systems.

proposals.title: Molecular Implementation of Precision: A Predictive Coding Model of Synaptic Protein and Neuromodulator Dynamics in Cortical Microcircuits
proposals.motivation: In the Free Energy Principle, precision weighting is fundamental to hierarchical Bayesian inference in the brain, determining the influence of prediction errors. While systems-level evidence from neuroimaging and dynamic causal modelling links precision to neuromodulators like acetylcholine and dopamine, the specific molecular and synaptic mechanisms that implement this weighting in local microcircuits remain unknown. Bridging this gap is critical for a mechanistic understanding of how regional dysfunction in precision estimation contributes to diseases like Alzheimer's, where neurotransmitter receptor densities are topographically altered.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that precision weighting in cortical microcircuits is implemented through the coordinated, activity-dependent regulation of synaptic proteins (e.g., NMDA receptor subunits, AMPA receptor trafficking proteins) and is dynamically modulated by the local binding kinetics of specific neuromodulators to their receptors, forming a molecular-level control system for prediction error gain.
proposals.method: We will develop a biophysically detailed microcircuit model using mean-field approximations and conductance-based neurons, incorporating dynamic neurotransmitter release and receptor kinetics (e.g., for D1, M1, and NMDA receptors). Synaptic efficacy will be governed by differential equations modelling the activity of proteins like PSD-95 and CaMKII. Precision will be cast as the inverse variance of prediction error units, with its dynamics controlled by the modelled molecular states. We will fit this model to empirical intracellular and local field potential data from rodent sensory cortex using variational Bayesian model inversion (as in Dynamic Causal Modelling) to infer the molecular parameter sets that best explain observed precision-like adaptations during mismatch tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first formal, testable model linking the molecular synaptic machinery to the computational principle of precision. It will generate concrete predictions for how specific receptoropathies (e.g., in Alzheimer's) disrupt predictive processing at the microcircuit level. The resulting 'molecular precision' model will serve as a foundational component for multi-scale whole-brain models of cognition and disease.
proposals.novelty: While existing work links neuromodulators to precision at a regional systems level, this proposal uniquely posits and formalizes the specific synaptic protein dynamics as the mechanistic substrate for precision control, moving from a descriptive correlation to a causal molecular model within the active inference framework.

proposals.title: A Hierarchical Active Inference Framework for Interpersonal Dysconnection in Social Psychopathology
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research in computational psychiatry, particularly under the dysconnection hypothesis, has successfully linked aberrant hierarchical Bayesian inference within a single brain to symptoms of disorders like schizophrenia. However, this framework has not been formally scaled to the interpersonal domain, where conditions like social anxiety and paranoia manifest as failures of belief updating in social interactions. This gap limits our understanding of how neural-level message passing failures translate to maladaptive social inference.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that social psychopathology arises from dysconnection in a multi-brain, hierarchical active inference system, where the precision-weighting of interpersonal prediction errors is pathologically altered, leading to characteristic failures in belief alignment and social learning.
proposals.method: We will construct a generative model of a dyadic interaction using a hierarchical Gaussian filter (HGF) architecture, where each agent maintains beliefs about the other's beliefs (i.e., a theory of mind). The model will be grounded in pre-2026 neurophysiology by parameterizing inter-regional effective connectivity (from DCM) as the precision of prediction errors at different hierarchical levels. We will fit this model to behavioural and multi-person magnetoencephalography (MEG) data from clinical and control groups performing a trust or belief-updating game, using variational Bayesian model inversion to estimate subject-specific parameters (e.g., expected precision) that quantify interpersonal dysconnection.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first formal, neurobiologically grounded model of social inference as a multi-agent active inference process. It will yield testable, mechanistic links between regional dysconnection, altered neuromodulation (via precision), and specific social cognitive deficits. This bridges the gap between individual brain dynamics and the interpersonal phenomenology of mental illness.
proposals.novelty: While active inference has been applied to social cognition, a direct formal extension of the neurophysiological dysconnection hypothesis to a multi-brain system, using coupled HGFs and grounded in pre-2026 DCM/neurotransmitter receptor maps, is a novel and non-obvious synthesis.

proposals.title: A Computational Account of Pathological Hyper-Integration: Excessive Belief Propagation as a Generative Mechanism in Neuropsychiatric Disease
proposals.motivation: The dysconnection hypothesis, central to the free-energy principle, posits that psychiatric and neurological disorders arise from aberrant functional integration and segregation in brain networks. While much research focuses on reduced or 'schismatic' connectivity, the dual pathology of excessive, rigid integration remains under-characterized. This hyper-integration may manifest as cognitive inflexibility, delusional beliefs, or stereotyped behaviors, yet lacks a formal generative model within the active inference framework.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific pathologies (e.g., delusions in schizophrenia, compulsions in OCD, cognitive rigidity in autism spectrum conditions) can be formally explained by a failure to down-regulate the precision or gain of belief propagation, leading to excessive and inflexible integration of information across hierarchical brain systems.
proposals.method: We will construct a hierarchical dynamic causal model (DCM) for M/EEG, where the gain on inter-regional message passing (i.e., the efficacy of forward and backward connections) is controlled by a hyper-parameter analogous to synaptic gain. Using spectral DCM and Bayesian model reduction, we will fit this model to existing longitudinal MEG datasets from Alzheimer's disease and schizophrenia cohorts. We will then simulate pathological hyper-integration by artificially increasing and fixing this gain parameter, predicting a shift in spectral phenotypes towards excessive cross-frequency coupling and reduced metastability, which will be tested against patient data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal, mechanistic model of hyper-integration as a pathological principle, complementing the dysconnection hypothesis. It will yield testable, quantitative predictions for spectral neurophysiology in specific disorders and suggest novel intervention targets aimed at restoring adaptive meta-stability. The model will be integrated into a whole-body-exposome framework to explore systemic contributors to aberrant gain control.
proposals.novelty: While dysconnection is well-studied, this proposal formally inverts the problem to model its dual. It moves beyond descriptive network 'hyper-connectivity' to propose a specific failure in the control of belief propagation precision as a generative cause, directly linking computational theory to neurophysiological phenotypes via DCM.

proposals.title: Beyond Bayesian Belief Updating: A Dynamical Systems and Information-Theoretic Re-interpretation of Markov Blanket Partitions in Brain Hierarchies
proposals.motivation: The Free Energy Principle (FEP) and Active Inference frameworks provide a dominant Bayesian interpretation of Markov blanket partitions, positing them as fundamental for variational belief updating. However, pre-2026 work in computational psychiatry and neurology highlights pathologies where Bayesian inference appears to break down, and alternative dynamical systems theories of brain function offer competing explanations for hierarchical segregation and integration. A rigorous, formal examination of Markov blankets through non-Bayesian lenses is crucial to test the universality of the FEP and to identify more fundamental principles of brain organization in health and disease.
proposals.hypothesis: The Markov blanket partition, as a structural and functional motif in brain hierarchies, can be formally derived and its dynamics explained using purely non-Bayesian frameworks—specifically, through attractor dynamics in coupled oscillators and information-theoretic constraints like the Information Bottleneck—without recourse to variational free energy minimization.
proposals.method: We will construct generative models of hierarchical brain systems using coupled differential equations (e.g., Wilson-Cowan or Kuramoto oscillators) and define Markov blankets via conditional independencies in their phase flow. Dynamics will be analyzed through the lens of synchronisation manifolds and attractor landscapes, not belief propagation. Concurrently, we will apply the Information Bottleneck method to empirical neuroimaging data (e.g., from the papers listed) to derive optimal partitions that trade off prediction and complexity, comparing these to anatomically defined Markov blankets. The core technical comparison will be between partitions and dynamics generated by these methods versus those derived from standard Active Inference simulations.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal, quantitative bridge between Active Inference and other major theoretical neuroscience paradigms, clarifying which aspects of Markov blankets are unique to Bayesian mechanics. It will yield novel, testable predictions for neurophysiological progression in diseases like Alzheimer's, where specific dynamical (e.g., bifurcation) rather than inferential failures may explain symptom onset. The results will establish a more general, framework-agnostic understanding of hierarchical segregation in complex systems.
proposals.novelty: While Markov blankets are inextricably linked to Bayesian inference in the FEP literature, systematically interpreting them through the formalisms of dynamical systems theory and the Information Bottleneck—which pre-2026 work has not done in this context—is a novel and potentially paradigm-challenging approach. It moves from asking 'what do blankets infer?' to 'what dynamical and informational constraints do blankets satisfy?'

proposals.title: A Free Energy Gradient: Quantifying Self-Evidencing in Physical and Biological Systems via Active Inference
proposals.motivation: The Free Energy Principle (FEP) posits that self-organizing systems resist disorder by minimizing variational free energy, a process termed 'self-evidencing'. While foundational to theoretical neurobiology and whole-body models, this remains a mathematical postulate lacking direct, system-agnostic empirical measures. Quantifying the degree of self-evidencing across scales—from cellular systems to brain regions in disease—would transform the FEP from a descriptive framework into a predictive, quantitative science for understanding resilience and failure modes in complex systems.
proposals.hypothesis: That the gradient of variational free energy with respect to system parameters or states can be empirically estimated from time-series data and serves as a universal, scalable measure of 'self-evidencing intensity', which will be attenuated in systems exhibiting pathology or impaired autonomy.
proposals.method: We will develop measures based on the temporal gradient of model evidence, approximated using dynamic causal modelling (DCM) and Bayesian filtering. For neurophysiological data (e.g., MEG from AD progression studies), we will use spectral DCM to estimate the free energy landscape of neural ensembles. For broader physical systems (e.g., coupled oscillators, organoids), we will employ generalized filtering and Bayesian mechanics to compute the free energy gradient from observed trajectories. The core technical innovation is the operationalization of the free energy gradient, ∂F/∂μ, as a time-varying, observable quantity.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first empirical toolkit to measure self-evidencing, enabling direct comparison of autonomous dynamics across systems. It will ground active inference in observable physics, offering new biomarkers for disease progression (e.g., quantifying the collapse of self-evidencing in Alzheimer's) and metrics for resilience in engineered or ecological systems.
proposals.novelty: While free energy itself is often computed, its gradient as a real-time, empirical measure of a system's 'striving' to maintain its model is novel. This moves beyond model comparison to continuous, within-system quantification of active inference, bridging the gap between FEP's intuition and control-theoretic evidence.

proposals.title: A Dual-Process Account of Neurodegeneration: Active Inference and the Minimization of Generative Model Complexity
proposals.motivation: The Free Energy Principle posits that biological systems minimize variational free energy, a bound on surprise, which entails a trade-off between accuracy and model complexity. In neurodegenerative diseases like Alzheimer's, progressive neuronal loss may reflect a pathological drive to minimize model complexity at the expense of accurate world models. This proposal investigates whether disease progression can be formalized as a dysregulated shift in this balance, compromising an organism's ability to maintain its existence within a viable phenotypic state.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the neurophysiological trajectory of Alzheimer's disease represents a pathological minimization of generative model complexity, driven by a failure of high-level (e.g., prefrontal) systems to exert hierarchical control over lower-level (e.g., sensory) model pruning, leading to a collapse in the system's capacity for adaptive, evidence-informed action.
proposals.method: We will construct whole-brain generative models using pre-2026 Dynamic Causal Modelling (DCM) for M/EEG, informed by pre-existing neurotransmitter receptor atlases, to simulate hierarchical Bayesian belief updating. Model complexity will be quantified using variational free energy decomposition. We will fit these models to longitudinal MEG data from Alzheimer's patients and controls, using Bayesian model reduction to track changes in effective connectivity and model complexity over time. The control aspect will be tested by comparing the integrity of top-down (prefrontal-to-sensory) versus bottom-up pathways.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel computational psychiatry account of neurodegeneration, framing symptom progression in terms of shifting Bayesian inference balances. It will yield testable, mechanistically interpretable biomarkers of disease stage based on inferred connectivity and complexity measures, moving beyond descriptive phenomenology.
proposals.novelty: While the Free Energy Principle is established, applying its complexity-minimization aspect as a primary driver of neurodegenerative pathophysiology, and formally testing it with longitudinal DCM on human neurophysiological data, is a novel and non-obvious synthesis of Friston's theoretical work with clinical neuroscience.

proposals.title: Hierarchical Predictive Coding in the Whole-Body Exposome: Linking Generative Model States to Neurophysiological Dynamics in Alzheimer's Disease
proposals.motivation: Current whole-body-exposome models for brain health are powerful but remain abstract, lacking direct links to the neurophysiological processes they aim to explain. This gap limits their utility for understanding the mechanistic pathways from systemic exposures to specific regional brain dysfunction in diseases like Alzheimer's. Grounding these models in empirical neural data is a critical step towards clinically actionable, mechanistically informed models of disease progression.
proposals.hypothesis: The hierarchical states of a whole-body generative (active inference) model, when fitted to multimodal exposome data, will show systematic and interpretable mappings to region-specific neurophysiological dynamics (e.g., spectral power and effective connectivity) measured with magnetoencephalography (MEG), and these mappings will be differentially disrupted along the Alzheimer's disease continuum.
proposals.method: We will employ a hierarchical Gaussian filter (a variational message passing scheme) to infer latent states from longitudinal exposome data (e.g., metabolic, inflammatory markers). These inferred state trajectories will then be used as regressors in Dynamic Causal Modelling (DCM) for cross-spectral densities, applied to resting-state MEG data from the same individuals (e.g., from the PREVENT-AD cohort). The DCM will estimate how exposome model states modulate regional neural excitability and effective connectivity within a canonical microcircuit model, providing a direct link from systemic to neural dynamics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first formal, quantitative link between whole-body exposome models and human neurophysiology, moving beyond correlation to a mechanistic integration. It will yield a novel, multi-scale model that explains how systemic factors 'get under the skull' to alter specific neural circuits, offering new, testable hypotheses about disease etiology and potential intervention points.
proposals.novelty: While hierarchical models and DCM are established, their integration to ground a whole-body, systemic model of health in fine-grained neurophysiology is unprecedented. The novelty lies in using the inferred states of a high-level physiological model as direct, time-varying modulators of a biophysical neural model, creating a closed loop between body and brain dynamics.

proposals.title: A Hierarchical Active Inference Model of Evidence Accumulation: Linking Neurotransmitter Gradients to Perceptual Decision-Making and Awareness
proposals.motivation: Drift-diffusion models (DDMs) provide a dominant account of perceptual decision-making as noisy evidence accumulation to a bound, but offer limited mechanistic insight into the neurobiological processes—such as neuromodulation and hierarchical message passing—that underpin both choice and the accompanying conscious awareness. Pre-2026 work, including Friston's own on topographic neurotransmitter receptor variation and its spectral consequences, suggests that regional neurophysiological heterogeneity fundamentally shapes belief updating. Integrating these perspectives is crucial for a unified theory of brain function in health and disease.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the dynamics of perceptual awareness during decision-making can be formalized as precision-weighted, hierarchical active inference, where the rate of evidence accumulation (drift rate) and decision threshold (bound) are emergent properties of context-sensitive, neuromodulatory control over prediction errors.
proposals.method: We will construct a hierarchical generative model within the active inference (POMDP) framework, where lower levels perform DDM-like accumulation of sensory evidence, and higher levels infer task context, modulating the precision (inverse variance) of prediction errors at lower levels. This effectively implements dynamic, Bayes-optimal control over the drift rate and bound. The model will be fitted to behavioural and intracranial EEG (iEEG) data from perceptual tasks, leveraging pre-2026 findings on regional receptor densities to constrain the spatial priors on precision parameters. Model comparison will use variational Bayesian methods (e.g., variational Laplace) and dynamic causal modelling (DCM) for cross-validation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, process-level bridge between descriptive cognitive models (DDM) and first-principles neurophysiological theories (active inference). It will yield testable predictions about how regional neurochemical gradients, evident in pre-2026 receptor mapping studies, determine individual differences in decision policies and their breakdown in neuropsychiatric conditions characterized by aberrant awareness.
proposals.novelty: While DDMs and active inference have been considered in parallel, their formal integration—where DDM parameters are directly derived from the optimization of variational free energy under neuroanatomically informed precision priors—is novel and moves beyond mere analogy to a mathematically unified framework.

proposals.title: A Multi-Agent Active Inference Framework for Social Metacognition and Shared Attention
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research in computational psychiatry and social neuroscience lacks formal models of how metacognitive beliefs—beliefs about one's own and others' beliefs—are generated and shared during interactions. This gap limits our understanding of social cognitive deficits in disorders like autism and schizophrenia, which are framed as dysfunctions in Bayesian belief updating. A scalable, multi-agent model of social metacognition is needed to bridge individual neurophysiology and collective behavior.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that shared attention and social metacognition emerge naturally from the hierarchical message passing of multi-scale, multi-agent active inference, where each agent maintains generative models of both the world and the belief-updating processes of other agents.
proposals.method: We will scale up single-brain Dynamic Causal Modelling (DCM) to a multi-agent system using a hierarchical active inference architecture. Each agent will be equipped with a generative model of a shared environment and a meta-generative model of other agents' inference processes (their precision-weighting and belief updating). Simulations will be conducted using the SPM toolbox and custom code for multi-agent message passing, with model evidence compared for scenarios of cooperative vs. competitive shared attention tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first formal active inference model of social metacognition, linking neurobiological principles (e.g., precision control) to group-level phenomena. It will generate testable predictions for hyperscanning EEG/MEG studies and offer a new computational framework for understanding the neurophysiological basis of social cognitive impairments.
proposals.novelty: While active inference has been applied to single minds and basic dyads, scaling it to a multi-agent system with explicit, recursive models of others' inference represents a significant conceptual and technical advance beyond pre-2026 work on 'theory of mind' or joint action.

proposals.title: Neural Implementation of Hierarchical Predictive Coding: A Multi-Scale Model Linking Microcircuits to Macroscopic Dynamics in Health and Alzheimer's Disease
proposals.motivation: The Free Energy Principle (FEP) provides a unifying theory of brain function as hierarchical Bayesian inference. However, a critical gap exists between the abstract mathematical formulation of the FEP and its implementation in specific, regionally heterogeneous neural circuits. Bridging this gap is essential for understanding how canonical computations break down in diseases like Alzheimer's, where neurophysiological progression shows characteristic topographic patterns.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the canonical microcircuit architecture for predictive coding can be parameterized by region-specific neurotransmitter receptor density profiles, and that the hierarchical progression of Alzheimer's disease can be modeled as a systematic perturbation of these microcircuit parameters, leading to predictable changes in macroscopic neurophysiological signals.
proposals.method: We will construct a multi-scale generative model. At the microcircuit level, we will use Dynamic Causal Modelling (DCM) for canonical microcircuits, where the synaptic parameters (e.g., excitatory and inhibitory connection strengths) are informed by pre-2026 regional neurotransmitter receptor density maps (e.g., from PET and autoradiography). These microcircuits will be arranged in a hierarchical architecture informed by structural connectivity. We will fit this model to empirical intracranial EEG (iEEG) and MEG data from healthy controls and individuals with Alzheimer's disease using variational Bayesian model inversion and evidence comparison.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete, testable link between the FEP and systems neuroscience, offering a mechanistic explanation for regional spectral differences in iEEG. It will generate a novel, pathophysiologically grounded model of Alzheimer's progression, moving beyond descriptive staging to a model of how synaptic and receptor-level changes produce the observed cascade of neurophysiological dysfunction.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in formally integrating pre-2026 empirical data on regional molecular neuroanatomy (receptor densities) directly into the parameters of a canonical microcircuit DCM, thereby grounding a universal computational principle in the biological heterogeneity of the brain and its disease-specific alterations.

ideas.title: Generative Bayesian Decoding of Stimuli from Oscillatory Power Manifolds
ideas.description: This approach moves beyond a deterministic reverse-GLM to a generative model that captures the full, probabilistic relationship between stimulus space and neural power features. It explicitly addresses the one-to-many mapping problem by learning a latent representation of stimuli constrained by both the observed power patterns and strong, empirically derived priors on stimulus properties. The system outputs not a single predicted stimulus, but a distribution over possible stimuli with quantified confidence, enabling robust decoding even under ambiguity. This framework inherently validates its predictions by assessing their plausibility within the learned generative model of the stimulus-neural response space.

ideas.title: Cortically-constrained computational duplication of sensorimotor loops
ideas.description: This research direction develops computational models of duplicated sensorimotor predictive loops, but with a key constraint: the architecture and oscillatory dynamics of the artificial agent's networks are designed to map onto source-localized human MEG/EEG data. The parametric oscillation analysis pipeline is used to extract spectro-spatial features (e.g., beta-band power in sensorimotor cortices) associated with specific behaviors in humans. These features then serve as both the design template and the validation target for the simulated agent, creating a closed loop where a model's expansion of behavioral repertoire is deemed plausible only if it reproduces the specific, source-localized oscillatory dynamics observed in the human brain during equivalent tasks.

ideas.title: Normative Transformations as a Reflexive Primitive for Predictive Coding
ideas.description: This research tests the hypothesis that canonical nonlinear transformations used to normalize neural data (e.g., log, Box-Cox, warping) are not merely analytical conveniences but may reflect fundamental, hardwired operations of reflexive sensory circuits. The project systematically compares how well different normative transformations, when embedded as the first stage in a hierarchical model, can account for neural responses to predictable versus unpredictable stimuli. By inverting the modeling primacy, we empirically determine if complex predictive tuning in higher areas is best explained as an elaboration of these reflexive normalization 'kernels,' rather than assuming prediction as the primary computational goal.

ideas.title: Synthetic neuroevolution of predictive elaboration from engineered reflexive archetypes
ideas.description: This research constructs a synthetic testbed to empirically determine how predictive control systems can be built upon non-predictive reflexive circuits. It begins by using evolutionary algorithms to develop complex, successful agent behaviors based solely on hardwired sensorimotor reflexes within environments that do not reward prediction. Once robust reflexive archetypes are established, the system introduces mutable, plastic circuits capable of learning temporal correlations. The key experiment observes whether these new predictive circuits parasitize, modify, or duplicate the existing reflexive pathways to improve performance when the environment is changed to reward anticipation. This provides causal evidence for the elaboration hypothesis free from biological entanglement.

ideas.title: Hierarchical Bayesian separation of neural and noise dependencies in time-frequency power
ideas.description: This approach develops a hierarchical Bayesian model to explicitly separate two sources of dependency in time-frequency power estimates: 1) structured, trial-to-trial neural dynamics related to the cognitive or behavioral variable of interest, and 2) generic, session-specific autocorrelation in measurement noise. The model uses informed priors to constrain the neural dependencies, promoting sparsity and temporal smoothness based on known neurophysiology. The system's output is a validated decomposition, where the inferred neural dependency structure is tested for its ability to predict held-out neural data and its correlation with behavioral measures, ensuring robustness and interpretability.

ideas.title: Reflexive Norms: When Small-N Reflexive Ensembles Fail to Converge to Predictive Averages
ideas.description: This research investigates the statistical foundations of the transition from reflexive to predictive control in neural systems. It applies the failure modes of the Central Limit Theorem for small-N ensembles to model populations of hardwired reflexive circuits, asking when their aggregated output (a 'power average') does not converge to a stable, predictable mean. This defines precise mathematical and empirical conditions under which predictive models cannot be reliable elaborations of underlying reflexes, forcing alternative cognitive architectures. The work thus challenges the primacy of prediction by identifying fundamental statistical limits to its derivation from reflexive substrates.

ideas.title: Phylogenetic State-Space Models for Evolving Neural Dynamics
ideas.description: This research direction integrates phylogenetic comparative methods with state-space models to study the evolution of neural dynamics. By embedding Kalman filters or related state-space estimators within a phylogenetic generalized linear mixed model (PGLMM) framework, one can model trial-by-trial neural power dynamics as evolving latent states. This allows for the statistical reconstruction of ancestral neural dynamic states—such as prediction error signals or attentional gain—across a clade of species. The synergy creates a novel capacity to test hypotheses about the evolutionary sequence of changes in real-time neural computation, bridging macro-evolutionary patterns with micro-scale trial-level dynamics.

ideas.title: Conditional Generative Inference for Single-Trial Oscillatory Phenomena
ideas.description: This project develops a framework for the probabilistic characterization of oscillatory power within individual experimental trials. Instead of attempting classical inference from a single datum, it employs a deep generative model—such as a conditional Variational Autoencoder—trained on a large corpus of multi-trial data to learn the joint distribution of trial features and their spectral properties. For any new single trial, the system infers a posterior distribution over its oscillatory power, conditioned on the trial's observable features and the learned population statistics. This provides robust, uncertainty-quantified estimates that allow researchers to state, for example, that a given trial's beta power is with 95% probability above a defined threshold, thereby enabling statistically grounded single-trial analysis without violating foundational statistical principles.

ideas.title: Structured, Shared Dynamic Motif Modeling for Within-Trial Non-Stationarity
ideas.description: This approach moves beyond simply relaxing stationarity to explicitly model the structured forms that within-trial neural dynamics take. It assumes that observed non-stationary power changes are generated by a sparse combination of a learned dictionary of smooth, prototypical temporal motifs (e.g., specific burst shapes or frequency modulation patterns). These motifs are inferred as latent variables shared across trials and conditions, providing a low-dimensional, interpretable representation of the dynamic repertoire. This framework robustly separates reproducible neural dynamics from unstructured noise, enabling generalization and direct testing of hypotheses about which dynamic motifs are engaged by different cognitive or behavioral states.

ideas.title: Joint Bayesian Source Estimation and Parametric Oscillation Modeling for MEG/EEG
ideas.description: This adversarially improved idea proposes a unified framework that jointly performs source reconstruction and parametric oscillation analysis in a single Bayesian estimation step. The system defines a generative model where source-level neural activity is explicitly modeled as a mixture of oscillatory components with spatially-varying parameters, which is then projected through the lead-field to produce sensor-level data. By inverting this model, it simultaneously estimates the most probable source locations and their oscillation characteristics, while providing credible intervals that account for both measurement noise and source localization ambiguity. This approach yields oscillation parameters that are intrinsically corrected for spatial leakage and are interpretable within the known limits of the MEG/EEG inverse problem.

ideas.title: Phylogenetic Reconstruction of Ancestral Cortical Oscillation Dynamics
ideas.description: This research direction applies phylogenetic comparative methods to source-localized MEG/EEG time-series data. By quantifying key parameters of cortical oscillations (e.g., peak frequency, power, cross-frequency coupling) from the source-reconstructed data of extant species, statistical models from evolutionary biology can be used to infer the probable oscillatory dynamics in the brains of ancestral species. This creates a novel evolutionary timeline of neurodynamic phenotypes, allowing hypotheses about how selective pressures shaped modern functional brain networks through their oscillatory signatures. The synergy lies in moving from comparing static model architectures to directly modeling the evolution of the dynamic neural processes that underlie cognition.

ideas.title: Adapt Bayesian State-Space Models from Ecology to Model Oscillatory Power Dynamics
ideas.description: This idea proposes developing a Bayesian alternative to frequentist GLMs for oscillatory power by drawing an analogy from population ecology. In ecology, Bayesian state-space models are a standard solution for analyzing non-normal, noisy time-series data like animal population counts, separating a latent biological process from observation error. We adapt this solution by treating neural oscillatory power as a latent state evolving over time/trials, with observed power as a noisy measurement. Using a Gamma or log-Normal process model within a GLM structure and MCMC inference, this approach would provide a robust framework for modeling non-normal power distributions, estimating the effects of experimental variables, and quantifying uncertainty in both the latent neural dynamics and model parameters.

ideas.title: A Generalized Circular-Linear Modeling Framework for Valid Inference on Neural Phase-Locking
ideas.description: This project develops a statistically valid modeling framework for neural phase-locking by moving beyond the standard GLM. It treats phase-locking values (e.g., PLV, coherence) as bounded, non-Gaussian observations and uses appropriate link functions (e.g., beta regression for bounded [0,1] measures, circular models for phase angles) within a GLMM. The framework explicitly incorporates the hierarchical and repeated-measures structure of neuroimaging data and validates inference using robust methods like parametric bootstrapping. This ensures that reported effects on phase synchronization are both detectable and statistically sound, overcoming the fundamental distributional mismatch of the original idea.

ideas.title: Adapting Financial Volatility Tracking to Neural Power Dynamics
ideas.description: This idea draws an analogy from financial econometrics, where state-space models like GARCH and stochastic volatility filters are used to estimate the latent, time-varying volatility (risk) of financial assets from noisy return data. The analogous solution involves modeling volatility as an unobserved stochastic process that evolves dynamically. Adapted to neuroscience, the research idea proposes to use a Kalman filter (or similar state-space estimator) within a Generalized Linear Model (GLM) framework to track a latent 'neural volatility' or 'excitability state' that drives trial-by-trial dynamics in measured oscillatory power (e.g., beta or gamma band). The model would treat the observed power on each trial as a noisy manifestation of this underlying, dynamically evolving state, providing a formal estimate of how neural population excitability fluctuates and adapts across an experimental session.

ideas.title: Modeling Neural Power Dependencies with Spatial Statistics
ideas.description: This idea inverts the common assumption of independent single-trial power by explicitly modeling dependencies between power estimates from adjacent time-frequency points or trials. The analogy is drawn from geostatistics and spatial ecology, where spatially autocorrelated data (like mineral deposits or species distributions) are analyzed using variograms and kriging. These methods quantify the covariance structure between nearby locations to improve estimation. Adapted to neuroscience, we treat the time-frequency-trial space as a multi-dimensional 'landscape' where neural power is measured. By constructing a variogram for this space, we can model how power covariance decays with 'distance' in time, frequency, and across trials. This model can then be used in a kriging framework to generate optimal, denoised power estimates that leverage the inherent dependencies, leading to more accurate statistical inference and reduced false positives compared to methods assuming independence.

ideas.title: Differentiated and Composable Sensorimotor Loops for Emergent Skill Hierarchies
ideas.description: This research investigates how duplicated predictive sensorimotor loops can be forced to specialize and compose to generate structured behavioral hierarchies, rather than redundant noise. We train an artificial agent where multiple internal models, derived from a duplicated base loop, are meta-optimized under a diversity-pressure objective that encourages specialization across different prediction tasks (e.g., short vs. long-term consequences, proprioception vs. exteroception). A soft, learnable arbitration network dynamically weights the contributions of these specialized loops based on the current goal and context, enabling the emergent composition of primitive predictions into complex, novel skills. The system is evaluated on its ability to generate a expanding, organized repertoire of behaviors that solve a curriculum of increasingly composite tasks in a physically grounded simulation.

ideas.title: A Hierarchical Bayesian Model for Neural Power Using Population Ecology Growth Curves
ideas.description: This idea extends the GLM for group-level power analysis by drawing an analogy from population ecology. In ecology, hierarchical models (e.g., mixed-effects von Bertalanffy models) are used to understand individual variability in growth trajectories within a population, estimating both population-level parameters and individual deviations. Adapted to neuroscience, we propose a hierarchical Bayesian model where each subject's neural power response (e.g., to a stimulus) is characterized by a subject-specific response function. The parameters defining this function (e.g., peak power, decay rate) are modeled as random effects drawn from group-level hyper-distributions. This directly quantifies inter-subject variability in power responses while robustly estimating the central group-level effect, moving beyond simplistic averaging or fixed-effects approaches.

ideas.title: Hierarchical Modeling of Neural Dynamics to Detect and Characterize Subgroup-Level Reorganizations
ideas.description: This research direction fuses a hierarchical GLM for group-level power analysis with the hypothesis of sudden architectural reorganizations. It proposes that major functional transitions may be preserved as distinct, discontinuous modes within the inter-subject variability of a population. The hierarchical model will be extended to explicitly test for and parameterize multiple latent subgroups, rather than assuming a single, continuous distribution of responses. This allows the detection of potential neurocognitive 'phenotypes' corresponding to different organizational regimes, moving beyond describing average group responses to uncovering evidence for discrete evolutionary or developmental jumps in circuit design.

ideas.title: Scaling via Emergent Order: Adapting Self-Organized Criticality from Statistical Physics to Neural Architectures
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the assumed necessity of hierarchical depth for scaling predictive power across spatial scales. By drawing an analogy from statistical physics, we adapt the concept of Self-Organized Criticality (SOC)—where complex systems like sandpiles develop scale-invariant behavior from simple local interactions, not a pre-defined hierarchy. The analogous solution is to design local rules that drive the system to a critical point, yielding emergent multi-scale phenomena. Adapted to machine learning, the research idea investigates whether non-hierarchical, homogeneous neural architectures can achieve similar multi-scale predictive power by incorporating learning dynamics or activation functions designed to push the network into a self-organized critical state. Success would be evidenced by the emergence of scale-invariant patterns in network activity and competitive performance on tasks requiring reasoning across spatial scales, without relying on engineered depth.

ideas.title: Robust online inference for non-stationary neural dynamics via Bayesian changepoint detection within GLMs
ideas.description: This system models trial-by-trial power dynamics by embedding a Generalized Linear Model (GLM) within a Bayesian online changepoint detection framework. It continuously infers the probability of a regime shift in the neural response properties, segmenting the data into epochs of stable dynamics. Within each inferred stable segment, it estimates the GLM parameters, allowing the model to adapt to abrupt, non-linear changes caused by learning, fatigue, or attention. This approach provides robust, online estimation of neural dynamics without assuming a fixed, pre-specified state transition model.

ideas.title: PhenoMix: Nonparametric Phenotype Discovery in Group-Level Neural Power Dynamics
ideas.description: This framework moves beyond a single-group hierarchical GLM by introducing a Bayesian nonparametric mixture model at the group level to discover latent subpopulations (phenotypes) in power responses. It clusters subjects based on their trial- or condition-level response profiles, automatically inferring the number of clusters from the data. For each discovered phenotype, it provides robust estimates of the characteristic response and within-phenotype variability, while also modeling subject-specific deviations. This approach directly addresses the heterogeneity of neural populations, leading to more interpretable and generalizable models of inter-subject variability.

ideas.title: Theory-constrained, generalizable modeling for high-density EEG/MEG arrays
ideas.description: This research develops a robust analysis framework for high-density sensor arrays that explicitly combats overfitting and spurious findings. It integrates generative neurophysiological models to provide biologically plausible constraints on the massively multivariate parameter space. The pipeline is designed to validate generalizability across experimental conditions, clinical cohorts, and imaging sessions, not just within a single dataset. The outcome is a principled methodology that yields interpretable, stable, and reproducible neural signatures from high-density EEG/MEG data.

ideas.title: A Formal Metric to Quantify the Continuum from Homeostatic Reaction to Allostatic Prediction
ideas.description: This research program moves beyond the qualitative debate by constructing a quantitative framework to describe regulatory strategies. It defines a set of measurable variables—such as the anticipatory lead time of a response, the weight of internal model-based predictions versus sensory error signals, and the energetic trade-off between preparation and correction—to compute a continuous Regulatory Spectrum Index (RSI). Experiments are designed to map how neural, endocrine, and immune systems shift their RSI under conditions of predictable versus unpredictable stress, learning, and aging. The outcome is a falsifiable, mechanistic taxonomy of physiological control that can identify when and why predictive strategies dominate, providing concrete insights into dysregulation in mental and metabolic diseases.

ideas.title: Inverse GLM Decoding via Geophysical Inversion Methods
ideas.description: This idea adapts solution strategies from geophysical inversion to solve the inverse problem of decoding experimental stimuli from observed oscillatory power. In geophysics, sparse and noisy field measurements (e.g., seismic waves) are used to infer hidden subsurface structures by inverting a physical forward model with added constraints. Similarly, we propose to invert the standard GLM (which predicts power from stimuli) using a regularized Bayesian framework. The oscillatory power serves as the measured field. The inversion will be stabilized by incorporating biologically plausible priors—such as expected temporal smoothness of stimuli, known response latencies, and bounds on stimulus intensity—transforming an ill-posed decoding problem into a tractable one that estimates the most probable experimental conditions given the neural data.

ideas.title: Surrogate-accelerated exact χ2 inference with guaranteed correction
ideas.description: This research designs a computationally tractable system for exact χ2 likelihood inference. It builds a fast, amortized neural surrogate model trained to approximate the non-central χ2 log-density and its gradient, enabling rapid exploration of the parameter space. The final inference is then corrected by re-weighting samples or refining estimates using direct, targeted evaluations of the exact likelihood, providing statistical guarantees. This approach makes exact χ2 inference practical for complex, high-dimensional models where direct calculation was previously impossible.

ideas.title: Punctuated Equilibria in Neural Architecture: Identifying Sudden Reorganizations via Developmental Critical Points
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption of gradual, continuous elaboration in brain evolution by drawing an analogy from evolutionary biology's theory of punctuated equilibrium. The analogous solution involved re-examining the fossil record to identify periods of stasis punctuated by rapid speciation events, often linked to environmental or developmental thresholds. Adapted to neuroscience, this research would seek evidence for sudden, systemic reorganizations in neural architecture—'neural punctuation'—rather than just incremental loop duplication. It would involve identifying specific genetic, metabolic, or connectivity critical points (e.g., in gene regulatory networks, energy allocation, or long-range projection density) that, when crossed, trigger a discontinuous leap in computational structure, such as the rapid establishment of a new cortical hierarchy or a global shift in network integration, potentially explaining major transitions in cognitive capacity.

ideas.title: Allostasis as a Control-Theoretic Regime Shift: From Error Correction to Anticipatory State-Space Navigation
ideas.description: This idea expands the challenge to homeostasis by drawing a direct analogy from control theory, which formally distinguished reactive error-correction (Classical Feedback Control) from anticipatory, model-based regulation (Model Predictive Control). The analogous solution was to define MPC as a fundamentally different paradigm based on optimization over a predictive horizon, not a mere tweak to feedback loops. Adapted to physiology, this research proposes to treat allostatic predictive control as a distinct regulatory principle—a biological MPC. The goal is to develop a formal framework where homeostasis maintains a set-point via deviation correction, while allostasis proactively navigates a multi-dimensional viability space using internal predictive models to optimize for future survival and efficiency. This shifts the question from 'how does allostasis extend homeostasis?' to 'under what conditions does an organism switch from a homeostatic to an allostatic control regime, and what are the formal signatures of each?'

ideas.title: Identify genomic and developmental mechanisms for saltational brain evolution
ideas.description: This research program moves beyond the untestable fossil hypothesis to identify concrete mechanisms for sudden architectural reorganization. It first uses computational models of brain development to define discrete, stable architectural states and the genetic regulatory changes needed to jump between them. It then performs comparative genomic analyses across lineages known for major brain transitions (e.g., stem mammals, birds) to search for evidence of these specific genetic mechanisms, such as duplications of key patterning genes or mutations in cis-regulatory elements. Finally, it validates candidate mechanisms by simulating their phenotypic effects in biologically grounded models of neural development, providing a testable, mechanistic basis for saltational change.

ideas.title: Adapt Seismic Tomography Inversion Methods for Robust Source-Space Oscillation Parameter Estimation
ideas.description: The original idea is to apply a parametric oscillation analysis pipeline to source-reconstructed MEG/EEG data, which is an ill-posed problem due to the inherent uncertainties in the source localization step. Drawing an analogy to geophysics, where seismic tomography solves a similar inverse problem to map the Earth's interior from surface waves, we can adapt its solution strategies. In seismology, regularization and Bayesian methods are used to incorporate prior models and quantify uncertainty. Adapting this, the research idea is to develop a framework that integrates anatomical (e.g., cortical manifold smoothness) and functional priors directly into the oscillation parameter estimation stage for source-space data. This would yield not only more robust maps of oscillatory dynamics but also spatially resolved confidence intervals for each estimated parameter (like frequency or power), significantly enhancing the interpretability and reliability of the analysis.

ideas.title: Adapting Wavelet-Based Signal Processing from Geophysics to Model Intra-Trial Oscillatory Dynamics
ideas.description: This idea expands the original seed by challenging the assumption of stationarity within a single trial through an analogy to geophysics. In seismology, the non-stationary nature of seismic signals is addressed using time-frequency decomposition techniques like wavelet transforms, which track how the energy of different frequencies changes over the course of an event. Adapting this solution, we propose to model oscillatory power dynamics in neuroscience by applying Continuous Wavelet Transform or matching pursuit algorithms to single-trial neural recordings. This approach generates high-resolution, trial-specific dynamic spectrograms, enabling the precise characterization of how oscillatory power in relevant frequency bands evolves during the cognitive process captured by the trial, moving beyond averaged or assumed stationary measures.

ideas.title: Adapting Phylogenetic Comparative Methods to Map Predictive Circuit Homology
ideas.description: This idea adapts the core methodology of phylogenetic comparative analysis from evolutionary biology to the problem of mapping predictive loops in connectome data. In phylogenetics, homologous traits are identified and their evolution traced across a species tree. Here, we treat brain regions or individual connectomes as operational 'taxa' and define sensorimotor loops (identified via network analysis) as connectional 'traits'. By constructing a circuit phylogeny based on similarities in connectional wiring patterns, we can apply comparative algorithms to distinguish homologous predictive circuits (sharing a developmental or genetic origin) from analogous ones (convergent wiring for similar predictive functions). This adapted method provides a formal framework to trace the duplication, divergence, and hierarchical organization of predictive loop motifs, moving beyond mere identification to an understanding of their potential evolutionary and developmental relationships within and across real brains.

It posits that in specific, high-pressure ecological niches—like those of tool-using corvids or cooperative breeding mammals—the relevant selective pressures are not population-wide averages but rare, extreme events whose statistical properties do not converge to normality. The novel synergy tests whether the cognitive capacity for long-term planning evolves as a 'fat-tailed' adaptation to these unpredictable, high-impact scenarios, explaining its patchy phylogenetic distribution better than models assuming normally distributed environmental challenges.

ideas.description: This research direction proposes using Generalized Linear Models (GLMs) with Gamma errors and log-link to model high-frequency, positive-skewed measures of metabolic or neural power expenditure. The key innovation is to frame the model's predictors and residuals not as deviations from a homeostatic set-point, but as signatures of allostatic predictive control. By comparing model fits where predictors represent anticipatory cues versus reactive error signals, the framework provides a rigorous statistical test to challenge whether allostasis is merely multimodal homeostasis or a fundamentally different principle governing energy regulation over time.

ideas.title: Adapting Bootstrap Particle Filtering for Single-Trial Oscillatory Inference
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from meteorology—ensemble forecasting for uncertainty quantification from a single initial state—to the problem of single-trial inference on oscillatory power. In weather prediction, an ensemble of model runs, each with slightly perturbed initial conditions or parameters, is launched from a single observed state to estimate forecast probabilities. Analogously, for a single neural trial, we can create a bootstrap ensemble of surrogate data series by resampling with replacement from the trial's time-frequency representation or by perturbing its phase and amplitude structure. This generates an empirical distribution of oscillatory power estimates for that specific trial. From this ensemble distribution, we can then compute confidence intervals, perform hypothesis tests against a baseline, or assess the statistical rarity of the observed power, thereby enabling proper statistical inference at the scale of a single experimental trial.

ideas.description: This research adapts the core solution from evolutionary biology's phylogenetic comparative methods (PCMs) to the original seed idea. The analogous problem in biology is determining how specific traits correlate with environmental pressures across species while accounting for shared evolutionary history. The solution uses known phylogenies and statistical models to isolate the signal of adaptive evolution. Adapted to cognitive science, the research would involve: 1) Building or selecting a well-supported species phylogeny for a cognitively diverse clade. 2) Quantifying 'temporal depth' through behavioral and cognitive proxies. 3) Quantifying key ecological and life-history variables from species' niches. 4) Using PCMs (e.g., phylogenetic generalized least squares, independent contrasts) to test for significant correlations between cognitive metrics and environmental pressures, thereby rigorously identifying the ecological niches that select for increased future-oriented planning.

The study measures the performance gap not just at convergence, but crucially in the data-scarce and out-of-distribution regimes, aiming to pinpoint if hierarchy is a necessary inductive bias for efficient scaling or merely a convenient implementation.

ideas.title: Adapting Structural Equation Modeling for Phase-Locking GLMs
ideas.description: This idea proposes extending the GLM framework to phase-locking measures by drawing an analogy from how Structural Equation Modeling (SEM) is used in social sciences and econometrics to analyze network relationships. In those fields, SEM solves the problem of testing causal hypotheses in complex systems by modeling latent variables (e.g., socio-economic status) linked to observed indicators, with GLM-like paths between the latent variables. The adapted solution is to treat measured phase-locking or coherence between neural signals as observed indicators of a latent 'functional connectivity' construct. The core GLM is then applied to predict this latent variable from experimental design matrices, enabling hypothesis-driven, parametric statistical testing on the strength and configuration of phase-based connectivity networks, moving beyond simple pairwise correlation tests.

ideas.title: Exact Likelihood Inference via Characteristic Functions
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from statistical physics, where exact characteristic functions are used to bypass intractable probability densities. The analogous problem is modeling a system's exact energy distribution, solved by working with the Fourier transform derived from the partition function. The adapted research idea is to develop statistical inference tools for models where the test statistic follows a χ2 distribution (e.g., in likelihood ratio tests, minimum χ2 estimation). Rather than approximating this distribution as normal or struggling with its exact PDF, we derive its exact characteristic function from the model's structure. New tools for parameter estimation, confidence sets, and model checking will then perform calculations (like evaluating tail probabilities or score functions) directly using this characteristic function via numerical integration or other transform techniques, providing exact inference without normality approximations.
