Radio telescopes solve the scaling problem using parametric self-calibration algorithms that iteratively model and correct for per-sensor errors and systemic distortions. Adapting this, the research will develop a novel parametric pipeline for neuroimaging that treats the sensor array as an interferometer. This pipeline will implement analogous iterative calibration models to correct for sensor variability, head model inaccuracies, and environmental noise, enabling the coherent fusion of hundreds of channels into a unified, high-fidelity reconstruction of brain activity, thus solving the scaling challenge for high-density EEG/MEG data.

This provides a quantitative, data-driven framework to challenge whether observed regulatory patterns are best explained by reactive homeostasis or failed predictive allostasis, moving the debate from theory to statistical inference.

ideas.title: Adapting Topological Data Analysis for Time-Frequency SPMs
ideas.description: This idea expands the original seed by drawing an analogy from astrophysics, specifically the analysis of Cosmic Microwave Background (CMB) maps. There, Random Field Theory (RFT) and Topological Data Analysis (TDA) methods like persistent homology are used to solve massive multiple comparison problems when searching for significant topological features (e.g., clusters of hot/cold spots) on a spherical manifold. We adapt this solution to the context of time-frequency SPMs in neuroscience. The adapted research idea is to apply TDA, in conjunction with RFT, to time-frequency SPMs. This involves using persistent homology to characterize the shape, size, and connectivity of significant blobs of oscillatory power in the time-frequency plane. This provides a novel multiple comparison correction framework that controls for false discoveries of topological features, offering a more powerful and interpretable method for identifying structured neural events compared to standard voxel-wise RFT thresholds.

ideas.title: Variance-Stabilized Gamma GLMs for Robust Power Spectrum Inference
ideas.description: This research direction fuses variance-stabilizing transforms (VSTs) with Gamma GLMs to create a robust analytical pipeline for power spectrum data. The core synergy is using the VST not as a replacement for the GLM, but as a diagnostic: if a VST successfully homogenizes variance, it informs the appropriate mean-variance relationship to specify within the Gamma GLM's variance function. This allows the GLM to be fit to the potentially more interpretable original data, but with parameters guided by the transform's stabilization performance. The result is a more principled, assumption-checking modeling workflow that enhances the reliability of inferences drawn from complex, non-stationary power estimates.

ideas.title: A Computational Taxonomy for the Failure Boundary of the CLT for Generalized Means
ideas.description: This research develops a rigorous computational framework to systematically map the conditions under which the Central Limit Theorem (CLT) fails for power means (generalized averages). It defines a formal, testable criterion for CLT failure based on the divergence of bootstrap-estimated confidence interval coverage from the nominal rate for small to moderate N. The study will classify probability distributions by their moment properties and simulate the convergence behavior of power means across a grid of exponent (p) and sample size (N) values. The outcome will be a predictive taxonomy and phase diagram that identifies the precise combinations of distribution, exponent, and sample size that necessitate non-normal inference models.

ideas.title: Validating parametric tests for low N via simulation-based calibration from computational physics
ideas.description: This research idea adapts the simulation-based calibration approach from computational physics to establish the lower limit of trial numbers where parametric tests remain valid. In molecular dynamics, researchers empirically determine the minimum system size for reliable property estimation by running many small-scale simulations with known parameters, rather than relying on asymptotic theory. Similarly, this project would involve generating a vast array of synthetic datasets (thousands per condition) from known, intentionally non-normal population distributions (e.g., exponential, log-normal, mixed) across a range of very low sample sizes (N=5 to 30). By applying standard parametric tests (like the t-test and ANOVA) to these datasets and comparing the resulting Type I error rates and statistical power to their nominal/theoretical values, we can empirically map a 'reliability landscape.' This landscape would define practical lower bounds for N based on the underlying distribution's deviation from normality and the desired error tolerance, providing a data-driven alternative to the theoretical assumptions of the central limit theorem for low trial counts.

ideas.title: A Benchmarking Framework for Normality Transforms in Power Data Using Principles from Chemometrics
ideas.description: This research adapts a systematic benchmarking approach from chemometrics to empirically compare nonlinear normality transformations for power data. In chemometrics, preprocessing techniques for spectral data are evaluated not just on their immediate statistical effect, but on their ultimate impact on the accuracy and robustness of a predictive calibration model. We propose an analogous framework: define a core power systems prediction task (like short-term load forecasting or photovoltaic output prediction), then create a pipeline to apply a wide range of candidate transforms (log, Box-Cox, Yeo-Johnson, arcsinh, quantile normalization, etc.) to the historical power data. Each transformed dataset is used to train and cross-validate a consistent forecasting model. The transforms are then ranked and compared based on the resulting forecast error (e.g., MAE, RMSE) and model stability, providing a practical, task-driven benchmark for normalization that moves beyond theoretical fit to a Gaussian distribution.

ideas.title: Apply GLM with Gamma errors and log-link to insurance claim severity modeling
ideas.description: This idea adapts the well-established actuarial science solution for modeling insurance claim severity—using generalized linear models with Gamma distribution and log-link—to the analysis of positive, skewed power data. In actuarial practice, this approach is preferred over normal models because it correctly handles strictly positive, right-skewed continuous outcomes like claim amounts, and the log-link provides multiplicative interpretability. For power research contexts (e.g., electricity consumption, solar panel output, or device power draw), this adapted method offers a statistically appropriate framework to model mean power as a function of predictors, account for heteroscedasticity where variance scales with the mean squared, and yield more reliable inferences and predictions than traditional linear models with normal errors.

ideas.title: Robust Outlier Detection with Regime-Switching Models for Power System GLMs
ideas.description: This approach moves beyond simple outlier down-weighting by formally testing whether anomalies in power data represent a distinct operational regime. It first applies robust statistical methods to flag potential outliers, then uses a likelihood-based framework (e.g., Markov-switching or finite mixture models) to determine if these points are best explained by a separate data-generating process. The model incorporates exogenous system state variables to inform regime probabilities, resulting in a hierarchical GLM that estimates parameters for both normal and event regimes. This provides not only robust fits but also interpretable models of rare but critical system states.

ideas.title: Apply Geophysics Signal Denoising via M-Estimation to Power Data
ideas.description: This idea expands the original seed by drawing an analogy from geophysics. In seismology, robust M-estimation techniques are used to handle high-amplitude, impulsive noise in seismic signals during inversion processes, preserving the integrity of the underlying geophysical model. The analogous solution involves integrating robust influence functions (e.g., Huber, Tukey) into the estimation algorithm to automatically down-weight outlier data points. Adapted to the power systems context, this approach involves modifying the maximum likelihood estimation within a GLM for power data (e.g., load, generation, or voltage data) by incorporating a similar robust objective function. This adaptation would enable the GLM to produce stable, reliable parameter estimates and forecasts even when the dataset contains outliers from sensor faults, transient events, or cyber-physical disturbances, without requiring pre-processing or arbitrary data removal, thereby enhancing the model's operational robustness and automation.

ideas.title: A Distribution-Aware Empirical Framework for Assessing Parametric Test Robustness at Low Sample Sizes
ideas.description: This project moves beyond the ill-posed question of a universal lower limit for CLT applicability. Instead, it develops a systematic simulation-based methodology to evaluate how the validity of common parametric tests (e.g., t-tests, ANOVA) degrades with decreasing sample size for different underlying data-generating distributions. The core system generates performance curves—mapping sample size and distributional features (like skew) to error rates in Type I error and statistical power—compared to non-parametric benchmarks. The final product is an open-source diagnostic software package that allows researchers to input their data's estimated properties and receive a evidence-based assessment of whether parametric inference is likely to be reliable for their specific low-n study.

ideas.title: Adopt variance-stabilizing transforms from low-light imaging for power spectral density estimation
ideas.description: This idea draws an analogy from low-light imaging, where variance-stabilizing transforms like the Anscombe transform are standard for handling signal-dependent Poisson noise. The analogous solution involves transforming the data to a domain with approximately constant variance, performing key analysis steps there, and then inverting the transform. Adapted to spectroscopy and signal processing, this method would apply similar transforms (e.g., Anscombe, Freeman-Tukey, or a derived transform for chi-square noise) to raw power spectral density estimates, which suffer from non-constant variance. This stabilization would enable more reliable application of Gaussian-based statistical techniques, smoothing algorithms, and regression models directly on the transformed data, leading to more accurate and interpretable results after the inverse transformation is applied.

ideas.title: Causal-Stability Framework for Evaluating and Selecting Normality Transformations
ideas.description: This research develops a principled framework to select normality transformations for power data based on both distributional fit and causal stability. It empirically compares nonlinear transforms but adds a critical validation layer: testing whether relationships modeled with the transformed data (e.g., between load and generation) are stable under perturbation, subsampling, or across different grid regions. The final output is a decision protocol that prioritizes transforms yielding not just normal residuals, but also robust, reproducible, and physically plausible models, moving beyond purely statistical criteria.

ideas.title: Robust, Non-Parametric Hybrid Framework for Real-Time Grid Oscillation Monitoring
ideas.description: This research designs and implements a real-time monitoring system for power system oscillations that is robust to the non-stationary and non-parametric nature of real-world grid data. The core innovation is an adaptive hybrid detector combining multiple lightweight, non-parametric algorithms (e.g., spectral change detection, sequential probability testing) whose outputs are fused by a meta-algorithm. This meta-learner continuously evaluates short-term detector performance and data quality to dynamically adjust decision thresholds and fusion weights. The resulting system provides reliable, low-latency anomaly alerts without relying on unstable parametric models, making it suitable for deployment in realistic, noisy grid environments with evolving dynamics.

ideas.title: Adaptive Sequential Probability Ratio Test for Real-Time Oscillation Monitoring
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution from seismology's earthquake early warning systems to scale the parametric test framework for real-time power oscillation monitoring. In seismology, adaptive Sequential Probability Ratio Tests (SPRT) are used on streaming sensor data to distinguish between normal background noise and the initial signals of an earthquake, enabling rapid, reliable alerts. The adapted idea implements a similar adaptive SPRT algorithm within the parametric framework. It will process real-time streaming phasor measurement unit (PMU) data, continuously comparing short data windows against a dynamically estimated background model of system oscillations. This provides a statistically rigorous, low-latency method for online detection of significant power changes, scaling the analysis to real-time monitoring and alerting for grid stability.

ideas.title: Adaptive, Noise-Model-Aware Variance Stabilization for Spectral Power Estimates
ideas.description: This system overcomes the rigidity of fixed transforms by implementing a two-stage adaptive pipeline. First, it segments the power spectrum and uses statistical diagnostics to locally estimate the dominant noise model (e.g., Poisson, Gaussian, mixed) and its parameters. Second, it selects or constructs an optimal variance-stabilizing transform for each segment—such as a generalized Anscombe for mixed Poisson-Gaussian noise or a Box-Cox transform with a locally fitted parameter. This ensures robust variance homogenization across the entire spectrum, even under non-stationary and heterogeneous noise conditions, leading to more reliable downstream analysis like peak detection or statistical testing.
